pages.title: Two-Dimensional Array Access Patterns
pages.summary: Optimal access patterns for 2D arrays in global memory require width alignment to warp size, typically achieved using cudaMallocPitch or cuMemAllocPitch to add padding.

pages.title: 64-Bit Compatibility
pages.summary: The 64-bit version of nvcc compiles device code in 64-bit mode, which requires that host code also be compiled in 64-bit mode.

pages.title: Accessing and Freeing Graph Memory Outside of the Allocating Graph
pages.summary: Graph memory allocations persist beyond the execution of the allocating graph and can be accessed by subsequent operations or other graphs, provided strict ordering via CUDA events, stream dependencies, or graph nodes is maintained before the memory is freed.

pages.title: Add Devices to Multicast Objects
pages.summary: Devices are added to a Multicast Team using cuMulticastAddDevice, a step that must be completed on all participating processes before binding memory to the Multicast Object.

pages.title: Addendums
pages.summary: The Addendums section of the CUDA C Programming Guide provides supplementary notes and specific behavioral details, such as those regarding the CUDA stream-ordered memory allocator.

pages.title: Allocating Multicast Objects
pages.summary: Multicast objects are created using cuMulticastCreate with a CUmemAllocationProp structure that specifies the number of devices and handle types, ensuring the allocation size meets the granularity requirements obtained via cuMulticastGetGranularity.

pages.title: Allocation and Deallocation Functions
pages.summary: User-defined operator new and delete functions cannot replace the built-in allocation and deallocation functions provided by the compiler for host or device code.

pages.title: Application Compatibility
pages.summary: Application compatibility in CUDA is managed by embedding binary and PTX code via compiler options, allowing runtime selection or JIT compilation based on device compute capability.

pages.title: Assertion in CUDA
pages.summary: Assertion support in CUDA devices (compute capability 2.x+) allows kernel execution to halt if an expression is zero, triggering debugger breakpoints or printing error messages to stderr.

pages.title: Asynchronous Data Copies (memcpy_async)
pages.summary: Asynchronous Data Copies (memcpy_async) is a CUDA 11+ feature that allows device code to explicitly manage asynchronous copying of data, enabling the overlap of computation with data movement through APIs in cuda/barrier, cuda/pipeline, and cooperative_groups/memcpy_async.h.

pages.title: Asynchronous Barrier (cuda::barrier)
pages.summary: The NVIDIA C++ standard library provides a GPU implementation of std::barrier via cuda::barrier, allowing for asynchronous arrive/wait synchronization with hardware acceleration on compute capability 8.0+.

pages.title: Asynchronous Concurrent Execution
pages.summary: CUDA exposes independent tasks such as host and device computation, and various memory transfers, which can operate concurrently depending on the device's compute capability and hardware resources.

pages.title: Atomic Functions
pages.summary: A reference for CUDA atomic operations, including generic and non-generic variants introduced in CUDA 12.8, legacy bitwise functions, address space predicates, alloca, compiler hints, and warp vote/match functions.

pages.title: barrier_arrive and barrier_wait
pages.summary: `barrier_arrive` and `barrier_wait` are member functions that provide a synchronization API similar to `cuda::barrier` within Cooperative Groups [CUDA_C_Programming_Guide:L12566-L12616]. These functions allow threads to perform independent

pages.title: barrier_arrive and barrier_wait
pages.summary: Member functions providing a synchronization API similar to cuda::barrier, allowing threads to hide synchronization latency by performing independent work between arrival and waiting.

pages.title: Binary Compatibility
pages.summary: Binary code (cubin) is architecture-specific and guaranteed to be compatible from one minor revision to the next (e.g., X.y to X.z where z >= y), but not across major revisions or to previous minor revisions, with support limited to desktop platforms.

pages.title: binary_partition
pages.summary: `binary_partition` is a collective operation within the CUDA Cooperative Groups (CG) library that partitions a parent group into one-dimensional subgroups. It is a specialized form of `labeled_partition` where the label is restricted to boo

pages.title: binary_partition
pages.summary: A collective operation in CUDA Cooperative Groups that partitions a parent group into two subgroups based on a boolean predicate, requiring Compute Capability 7.0.

pages.title: Bind Memory to Multicast Objects
pages.summary: Physical memory allocated with cuMemCreate is bound to a Multicast Handle using cuMulticastBindMem for each participating device.

pages.title: Breakpoint Function
pages.summary: The __brkpt() function suspends the execution of a kernel function when called from any device thread.

pages.title: Built-in Variables
pages.summary: Built-in variables provide information about grid and block dimensions and thread indices, and are valid only in device code.

pages.title: Built-in Vector Types
pages.summary: CUDA provides built-in vector types derived from basic integer and floating-point types, allowing access to components via x, y, z, and w fields, along with specific constructor functions and alignment rules.

pages.title: Call Stack
pages.summary: The call stack size on CUDA devices of compute capability 2.x and higher can be queried and configured using specific runtime API functions, with overflow handling and compiler warnings managed through debugger integration and static analysis.

pages.title: CDP1 Constant Memory
pages.summary: In CUDA Dynamic Parallelism version 1, constant memory is immutable from the device and is automatically inherited by child kernels from their parents.

pages.title: CDP1 CUDA C++ Reference
pages.summary: Describes the CDP1 language interface and API, noting its similarity to the host runtime and per-thread nature.

pages.title: CDP1 Device-Side Kernel Launch
pages.summary: Details the <<<>>> syntax for launching kernels from the device in CDP1, including parameters for grid, block, shared memory, and stream.

pages.title: CDP1 Device Management
pages.summary: The CDP1 device runtime lacks multi-GPU support and operates exclusively on the currently executing device, though it allows querying properties of all CUDA-capable devices.

pages.title: CDP1 Execution Environment
pages.summary: The CDP1 execution environment and memory model details are documented in the main Execution Environment and Memory Model section, with specific CDP2 variations noted separately.

pages.title: CDP1 Execution Environment Details
pages.summary: Details the CUDA execution model primitives in the context of CDP1, including the deprecation warning for explicit synchronization.

pages.title: CDP1 Global Memory
pages.summary: Details coherence and consistency guarantees for global memory between parent and child grids in CDP1, specifying visibility points and synchronization requirements.

pages.title: CDP1 Launch Environment Configuration
pages.summary: In CDP1, child kernels inherit global device configurations from their parent and cannot be reconfigured from the device.

pages.title: CDP1 Launches are Asynchronous
pages.summary: In CUDA Dynamic Parallelism version 1 (CDP1), device-side kernel launches are asynchronous, allowing the parent thread to continue execution immediately without waiting for child kernels to complete.

pages.title: Legacy CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1)
pages.summary: This section serves as a reference for the legacy CDP1 implementation, directing users to the CDP2 documentation for current capabilities.

pages.title: CDP1 Local Memory
pages.summary: In CUDA Dynamic Parallelism version 1 (CDP1), local memory is private to a thread and cannot be passed as an argument to child kernels; such storage must instead be allocated from global memory.

pages.title: CDP1 Memory Model
pages.summary: The CDP1 memory model directs users to the general memory model documentation, with specific details for the CDP2 version located elsewhere in the guide.

pages.title: CDP1 Ordering and Concurrency
pages.summary: Explains ordering semantics (in-order within stream) and the lack of concurrency guarantees between thread blocks in CDP1.

pages.title: CDP1 Parent and Child Grids
pages.summary: CDP1 defines a properly nested execution model where a parent grid's completion is implicitly synchronized with the completion of all child grids it launches.

pages.title: CDP1 Programming Interface
pages.summary: The CDP1 programming interface is a specific version of the CUDA Dynamic Parallelism API, with detailed documentation referenced under the general Programming Interface section.

pages.title: CDP1 Scope of CUDA Primitives
pages.summary: Describes the scope of streams and events in CDP1 (thread block scope) and the undefined behavior if used outside that scope.

pages.title: CDP1 Shared and Local Memory
pages.summary: In CUDA Dynamic Parallelism version 1 (CDP1), shared and local memory are private to thread blocks and threads respectively, are not visible or coherent between parent and child kernels, and referencing them outside their scope is undefined behavior.

pages.title: CDP1 Streams and Events
pages.summary: In CDP1, device-created streams and events operate within thread block scope with undefined behavior for cross-block usage, while host-created streams and events are invalid within kernels.

pages.title: CDP1 Synchronization
pages.summary: Details the deprecation of explicit synchronization with child kernels in CDP1 and explains the implicit synchronization behavior triggered when a parent block exits before its child launches complete.

pages.title: CDP1 Texture Memory
pages.summary: Details coherence rules for texture memory between parent and child grids in CDP1, noting that coherence is enforced at grid invocation and completion.

pages.title: CDP1 Zero Copy Memory
pages.summary: Zero-copy memory shares the same coherence and consistency model as global memory and cannot be allocated or freed by device code.

pages.title: CDP2 Compatibility and Interoperability
pages.summary: Explains compatibility rules, default behavior, and restrictions on mixing CDP1 and CDP2 functions in the same call graph.

pages.title: CDP2 Differences
pages.summary: CDP2 removes explicit device-side synchronization and virtualized pools, introduces per-grid stream tracking and tail/fire-and-forget streams, and is restricted to 64-bit compilation.

pages.title: CDP2 vs CDP1
pages.summary: This section summarizes the differences, compatibility, and interoperability between the new CDP2 and legacy CDP1 CUDA Dynamic Parallelism interfaces, including instructions for opting out of CDP2 on devices with compute capability less than 9.0.

pages.title: Choosing the Right Page Size
pages.summary: Details the trade-offs of small vs. large page sizes: small pages reduce fragmentation but increase TLB misses; large pages reduce TLB misses but increase migration latency and fragmentation, with a strong recommendation to tune virtual rather than physical page sizes.

pages.title: __cluster_dims__
pages.summary: A compile-time specifier for thread block cluster dimensions in CUDA kernels, available for Compute Capability 9.0 and above.

pages.title: Cluster Launch Control
pages.summary: Cluster Launch Control, introduced in Compute Capability 10.0, is a CUDA feature that enables thread blocks to cancel the launch of other pending thread blocks, facilitating work-stealing while maintaining reduced launch overheads and supporting preemption.

pages.title: Cluster Occupancy API
pages.summary: The Cluster Occupancy API provides functions and configuration structures to query maximum active clusters and potential cluster sizes, enabling the configuration of cluster-based kernel launches with specific dimensions.

pages.title: Coherency and Concurrency (Pre-6.x)
pages.summary: On pre-6.x GPUs, simultaneous CPU and GPU access to managed memory is prohibited to guarantee coherency, meaning the CPU cannot access allocations while the GPU is active.

pages.title: Compressible Memory
pages.summary: Compressible memory accelerates accesses to data with unstructured sparsity by saving DRAM and L2 bandwidth and capacity, allocated via CU_MEM_ALLOCATION_COMP_GENERIC and verified through device attribute queries.

pages.title: CUDA Compute Modes
pages.summary: CUDA compute modes (Default, Exclusive-process, and Prohibited) control device access granularity, while Compute Preemption on Pascal and later architectures allows instruction-level task switching to prevent monopolization.

pages.title: Concurrent Access Rules and Examples
pages.summary: Defines the rules for concurrent access between CPU and GPU, and between multiple GPU kernels, when using managed and non-managed memory.

pages.title: Conditional Graph Nodes
pages.summary: Conditional graph nodes enable dynamic control flow, including IF, WHILE, and SWITCH logic, within CUDA graphs by evaluating device-set condition values to determine execution paths.

pages.title: Conditional Handles
pages.summary: Conditional handles (cudaGraphConditionalHandle) represent condition values in CUDA graphs, created via cudaGraphConditionalHandleCreate, associated with a single node, and optionally initialized with a default value at the start of each execution.

pages.title: Conditional IF Nodes
pages.summary: Conditional IF nodes execute a body graph when a condition is non-zero, and optionally a second body graph when the condition is zero, introduced in CUDA 12.8.

pages.title: Conditional Node Body Graph Requirements
pages.summary: Defines the constraints for graphs used as conditional node bodies, including single-device residency, allowed node types, and restrictions on dynamic parallelism and memory access.

pages.title: Conditional SWITCH Nodes
pages.summary: SWITCH nodes, introduced in CUDA 12.8, allow a conditional node to execute one of n different subgraphs based on an evaluated condition value, where the nth graph is selected if the value equals n, and no graph is executed if the value is greater than or equal to n.

pages.title: Conditional WHILE Nodes
pages.summary: The WHILE conditional node type executes its body graph repeatedly as long as the condition remains non-zero, with evaluation occurring at execution start and after each body completion.

pages.title: Constant Memory
pages.summary: Constant memory is a device memory space cached in the constant cache, where read requests are split based on distinct memory addresses, affecting throughput depending on cache hits.

pages.title: Controlling Access Rights
pages.summary: Applications use the cuMemSetAccess function within the Virtual Memory Management APIs to explicitly grant or restrict device access to specific virtual address ranges, providing fine-grained control with minimal overhead compared to global peer access settings.

pages.title: Cooperative Groups
pages.summary: Cooperative Groups is an extension to the CUDA programming model introduced in CUDA 9 that allows developers to organize and synchronize communicating threads at granularities beyond the single thread block barrier.

pages.title: C++ Compatibility
pages.summary: The CUDA compiler front end processes source files according to C++ syntax rules, supporting full C++ for host code but only a subset for device code.

pages.title: CPU Virtual Memory
pages.summary: When using CUDA stream-ordered memory allocator APIs, avoid setting VRAM limitations with “ulimit -v” as this is not supported.

pages.title: cuGraphAddMemsetNode
pages.summary: The cuGraphAddMemsetNode API does not support memory allocated via the stream ordered allocator, although memsets of such allocations can still be stream captured.

pages.title: cuPointerGetAttribute Query
pages.summary: Invoking cuPointerGetAttribute on an allocation after invoking cudaFreeAsync on it results in undefined behavior, regardless of stream accessibility.

pages.title: cuGetProcAddress Error Handling
pages.summary: Details on handling cuGetProcAddress failures, distinguishing between API/usage errors (CUresult) and symbol lookup errors (CUdriverProcAddressQueryResult). Explains CU_GET_PROC_ADDRESS_VERSION_NOT_SUFFICIENT and CU_GET_PROC_ADDRESS_SYMBOL_NOT_FOUND.

pages.title: Guidelines for cuGetProcAddress
pages.summary: Guidelines for using the cuGetProcAddress API, including matching CUDA version typedefs and checking driver version sufficiency before calling.

pages.title: Cubemap Layered Surfaces
pages.summary: Cubemap layered surfaces are accessed as two-dimensional layered surfaces using specific read and write functions with face indices and texture coordinates.

pages.title: Cubemap Layered Textures
pages.summary: Cubemap layered textures are layered CUDA arrays where each layer is a cubemap of the same dimension, addressed via an integer layer index and three floating-point coordinates, supported on compute capability 2.0+ devices.

pages.title: Cubemap Surfaces
pages.summary: Cubemap surfaces are accessed as two-dimensional layered surfaces using surfCubemapread and surfCubemapwrite, where an integer index selects the face and floating-point coordinates address the texel.

pages.title: Cubemap Textures
pages.summary: Cubemap textures are six-layered 2D textures addressed by direction vectors, supported on compute capability 2.0+ devices via specific CUDA array and texture fetch APIs.

pages.title: Anonymous Unions in CUDA
pages.summary: Member variables of a namespace scope anonymous union cannot be referenced in a __global__ or __device__ function.

pages.title: CUDA Arrays
pages.summary: CUDA arrays are opaque, dimensionally flexible memory layouts optimized for texture fetching, accessible only via texture or surface references.

pages.title: CUDA Asynchronous Programming
pages.summary: An introduction to the asynchronous programming model for memory operations, starting with Ampere architecture.

pages.title: CUDA Atomic Arithmetic Functions
pages.summary: Section 10.14.1 of the CUDA C Programming Guide introduces arithmetic atomic operations, including atomicAdd, atomicSub, atomicMin, atomicMax, atomicInc, and atomicDec.

pages.title: CUDA Atomic Functions Overview
pages.summary: CUDA atomic functions perform read-modify-write operations on 32-bit, 64-bit, or 128-bit words in global or shared memory, supporting various scopes and memory orders.

pages.title: CUDA C++ Language Support
pages.summary: CUDA C++ language support provides a mix of host and device code compilation via nvcc, supporting C++11, C++14, C++17, and C++20 features with specific restrictions on standard libraries, exceptions, and device-specific attributes.

pages.title: CUDA C++17 and C++20 Language Features
pages.summary: NVIDIA's nvcc compiler supports C++17 features starting from version 11.0 and C++20 features starting from version 12.0, with specific restrictions detailed in the official documentation.

pages.title: CUDA C++17 Code Samples: Classes, Templates, and Functors
pages.summary: Code examples demonstrating the use of __device__ classes, inheritance, templates, and functors in CUDA C++17, including PixelRGBA aggregation, Shape/Point inheritance, and template-based vector operations.

pages.title: CUDA C++17 constexpr Host/Device Interaction Rules
pages.summary: Rules governing the generation and execution of constexpr functions marked with __host__ or __device__ qualifiers in CUDA C++17, including restrictions on ODR-use, exceptions, and RTTI when called across host/device boundaries.

pages.title: Classes in CUDA
pages.summary: CUDA supports C++ classes with specific restrictions, notably prohibiting non-const static data members and preventing static member functions from being declared as __global__.

pages.title: CUDA Compute Capabilities
pages.summary: CUDA Compute Capability is a versioning scheme (X.Y) that identifies GPU hardware features, architecture generations, and instruction sets available to applications at runtime.

pages.title: CUDA Compute Capabilities Overview
pages.summary: This page provides an overview of CUDA compute capabilities, detailing how device specifications and features vary by capability version, and links to specific architecture documentation and IEEE floating-point compliance standards.

pages.title: CUDA Compute Capability Technical Specifications
pages.summary: Technical specifications and feature support tables for various CUDA compute capabilities (7.x through 12.0), including atomic functions, memory limits, register counts, and texture/surface dimensions.

pages.title: CUDA Configuration Options
pages.summary: CUDA configuration options allow host programs to control device runtime resource allocation, such as pending launch buffers and thread stack sizes, using the cudaDeviceSetLimit API before kernel execution.

pages.title: Const and Pure GNU Attributes in CUDA
pages.summary: The const and pure GNU attributes are supported for host and device functions to inform the optimizer about function side effects, with pure indicating no mutable state changes and const indicating no access or changes to mutable state.

pages.title: Const-Qualified Variables in CUDA
pages.summary: Namespace scope or static member const variables without execution space annotations are treated as host variables; their values can be used in device code if initialized with constant expressions and are of built-in floating point (except MSVC) or integral types, but references or addresses cannot be taken in device code.

pages.title: Constexpr Functions in CUDA
pages.summary: By default, constexpr functions cannot be called from functions with incompatible execution spaces, but the --expt-relaxed-constexpr flag removes this restriction to allow cross-execution space calls.

pages.title: Constexpr Variables in CUDA
pages.summary: Namespace scope or static member constexpr variables without execution space annotations are treated as host variables, allowing their values to be used in device code under specific scalar type constraints while prohibiting references or addresses.

pages.title: CUDA Context
pages.summary: A CUDA context is analogous to a CPU process, encapsulating all resources and actions performed within the driver API, with each context maintaining its own distinct address space and managed via a stack per host thread.

pages.title: C++11 Features in CUDA
pages.summary: nvcc supports C++11 features enabled by the host compiler and can enable all C++11 features via the -std=c++11 flag, which configures the host toolchain accordingly.

pages.title: C++14 Features in CUDA
pages.summary: nvcc supports C++14 features enabled by the host compiler, activated via the -std=c++14 flag which configures the entire host toolchain to use the C++14 dialect.

pages.title: CUDA __CUDA_ARCH__ Preprocessor Symbol
pages.summary: The __CUDA_ARCH__ preprocessor symbol enables device-side conditional compilation, but strict rules prohibit its use in type signatures, external linkage definitions, and shared headers to ensure consistent behavior across host and device code and separate compilation units.

pages.title: CUDA Direct3D 11 Buffer Mapping
pages.summary: This page explains how to map a device pointer onto an imported Direct3D 11 memory object using cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedBuffer, ensuring offset and size constraints are met and resources are properly freed.

pages.title: CUDA Direct3D 11 Device LUID Matching
pages.summary: Explains that CUDA and Direct3D 11 devices must match via LUID comparison to import memory/synchronization objects. Provides code to query IDXGIDevice, IDXGIAdapter, and CUDA device properties to find the matching CUDA device.

pages.title: CUDA Direct3D 11 Memory Import
pages.summary: This page details the creation of shareable Direct3D 11 resources and their subsequent import into CUDA using `cudaImportExternalMemory` with dedicated memory flags.

pages.title: CUDA Direct3D 11 Mipmapped Array Mapping
pages.summary: This page details the process of mapping CUDA mipmapped arrays onto imported Direct3D 11 memory objects, including parameter conversion and resource flag handling.

pages.title: CUDA Direct3D 11 Synchronization Object Import
pages.summary: This page describes how to import shareable Direct3D 11 fence and keyed mutex synchronization objects into CUDA using NT handles, named handles, or KMT handles via the CUDA External Memory/Semaphore API.

pages.title: CUDA Direct3D 11 Synchronization Signaling and Waiting
pages.summary: This page details the mechanisms for signaling and waiting on imported Direct3D 11 fence and keyed mutex objects using CUDA external semaphores, including API usage and ordering constraints.

pages.title: Mapping Buffers onto Imported D3D12 Memory Objects
pages.summary: This page explains how to map a device pointer onto an imported Direct3D 12 memory object using cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedBuffer and notes that the mapped pointer must be freed with cudaFree().

pages.title: Direct3D 12 Device LUID Matching
pages.summary: Explains how to determine the corresponding CUDA device for a Direct3D 12 device by comparing their Local Unique Identifiers (LUIDs) to ensure correct memory and synchronization object interoperability.

pages.title: Importing Direct32 Fence Objects into CUDA
pages.summary: Explains how to import shareable Direct3D 12 fence objects into CUDA using NT handles (regular and named) and the associated resource management requirements.

pages.title: Signaling and Waiting on Imported D3D12 Fence Objects
pages.summary: Describes how to signal and wait on imported Direct3D 12 fence objects from CUDA using `cudaSignalExternalSemaphoresAsync` and `cudaWaitExternalSemaphoresAsync`, emphasizing the strict ordering and domain requirements for corresponding D3D12 operations.

pages.title: Importing Direct3D 12 Memory Objects into CUDA
pages.summary: Details how to import shareable Direct3D 12 heap and committed resource memory objects into CUDA using NT handles (both regular and named), including handle ownership and the cudaExternalMemoryDedicated flag.

pages.title: Mapping Mipmapped Arrays onto Imported D3D12 Memory Objects
pages.summary: Details how to map a CUDA mipmapped array onto an imported Direct3D 12 memory object, including helper functions for converting DXGI formats to CUDA channel formats, D3D12 extents to CUDA extents, and D3D12 resource flags to CUDA array flags.

pages.title: Deduced Return Type in CUDA
pages.summary: __global__ functions cannot have deduced return types, and __device__ functions with deduced return types are converted to void by the frontend compiler, preventing their use in host code.

pages.title: Defaulted Functions in CUDA
pages.summary: In CUDA, execution space specifiers on non-virtual functions explicitly defaulted on their first declaration are ignored and inferred, whereas specifiers are preserved if the function is virtual or defaulted on a subsequent declaration.

pages.title: Deprecation Annotation in CUDA
pages.summary: The CUDA compiler (nvcc) supports standard and vendor-specific deprecation attributes for both host and device code, generating diagnostics during compilation that can be suppressed via specific flags or pragmas.

pages.title: CUDA Device API Reference
pages.summary: This page lists the CUDA Runtime API functions supported in the device runtime, detailing specific behavioral differences from host execution such as per-thread error states and restricted stream/event flags.

pages.title: CUDA Device Constant and Device Memory
pages.summary: Explains that __device__ and __constant__ memory declared at file scope behave identically in device runtime, with all kernels having the same view of these variables.

pages.title: CUDA Device Errors and Launch Failures
pages.summary: CUDA error handling relies on per-thread error codes retrieved via cudaGetLastError(), where device-side launch failures and exceptions (such as invalid address access) are propagated back to the host.

pages.title: CUDA Device Events
pages.summary: CUDA device runtime events provide limited support for inter-stream synchronization via cudaStreamWaitEvent, requiring the use of cudaEventDisableTiming as timing queries are unsupported.

pages.title: CUDA Device Management
pages.summary: Device runtime code is restricted to managing only the active GPU, with specific limitations on device switching and property querying APIs.

pages.title: CUDA Device Streams
pages.summary: CUDA device streams allow for concurrent execution of kernels within a grid, requiring the use of non-blocking flags and tail launches for synchronization due to the lack of host-side synchronization APIs.

pages.title: CUDA Device Synchronization
pages.summary: Explicit inter-thread synchronization from a parent thread to child grids is not supported, meaning visibility of changes in child grids to the parent grid cannot be guaranteed without explicit mechanisms like CUDA Events.

pages.title: CUDA Direct3D 10 Interoperability
pages.summary: CUDA Direct3D 10 interoperability allows sharing graphics resources between Direct3D 10 and CUDA by registering, mapping, and unregistering resources to enable GPU-accelerated data processing.

pages.title: CUDA Direct3D 11 Interoperability
pages.summary: CUDA Direct3D 11 interoperability allows sharing resources between CUDA and Direct3D 11 by registering D3D11 resources with the CUDA graphics API, mapping them for kernel access, and unregistering them upon completion.

pages.title: CUDA Driver API
pages.summary: The CUDA Driver API is a low-level C API that provides direct control over CUDA contexts and modules, serving as the foundation for the higher-level CUDA Runtime API.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism
pages.summary: An extension to the CUDA programming model enabling a CUDA kernel to create and synchronize with new work directly on the GPU.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) API Errors
pages.summary: Error handling in CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) involves per-thread error codes via cudaGetLastError() for launch failures and host-side reporting via cudaDeviceSynchronize() for device-side exceptions.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) API Reference
pages.summary: Overview of the subset of the CUDA Runtime API supported in device code for CUDA Dynamic Parallelism version 1, detailing synchronization, memory, and stream operations with specific semantic differences from host execution.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) Basics
pages.summary: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) allows device code to launch and synchronize with child kernels, utilizing a device runtime that is a functional subset of the host runtime.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) cudaGetParameterBuffer
pages.summary: Details on the cudaGetParameterBuffer API in CDP1, used to obtain a buffer for kernel parameters, including PTX and CUDA-level declarations and alignment requirements.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) cudaLaunchDevice
pages.summary: Details on `cudaLaunchDevice` API in CDP1, including PTX and CUDA-level declarations, parameter buffers, and launch configuration.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) Compiling and Linking
pages.summary: Compiling and linking CUDA programs with Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) requires the `-rdc=true` flag and automatic or explicit linking against the static device runtime library `libcudadevrt`.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) Configuration Options
pages.summary: Configurable limits for CDP1 including sync depth, pending launch count, and stack size, managed via the cudaDeviceSetLimit() API.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) Device Management
pages.summary: Device management in CDP1 restricts control to the currently executing device, disabling cudaSetDevice and cudaGetDeviceProperties while supporting cudaGetDevice and cudaDeviceGetAttribute.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) ECC Errors
pages.summary: ECC error handling in CDP1 involves no in-kernel notification, with errors reported at the host side after the launch tree completes, potentially generating exceptions or continuing execution based on configuration.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) Events
pages.summary: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism version 1 (CDP1) supports only inter-stream synchronization via `cudaStreamWaitEvent`, requiring events to be created with `cudaEventDisableTiming` and restricting handle scope to the creating thread block.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) Including Device Runtime API
pages.summary: Prototypes for the CUDA device runtime API are included automatically during program compilation, eliminating the need to explicitly include the cuda_device_runtime_api.h header.
aliases: Including Device Runtime API in CUDA Code (CDP1)

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) Kernel Overhead
pages.summary: Overhead imposed by device runtime execution tracking when dynamic launches are active, potentially decreasing performance for library calls made from the device compared to the host side.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) Launch Setup APIs
pages.summary: Low-level launch APIs in CDP1. `cudaGetParameterBuffer` and `cudaLaunchDevice` are exposed via PTX and device runtime. These allow direct kernel launch from device code without the `<<<>>>` syntax.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) Memory Allocation
pages.summary: In CUDA Dynamic Parallelism 1, device-side memory allocation uses a separate heap managed by `cudaLimitMallocHeapSize`, and cross-boundary free operations between host and device are prohibited.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) Memory Declarations
pages.summary: In CUDA Dynamic Parallelism version 1, file-scope `__device__` and `__constant__` variables behave identically to host launches, allowing all kernels to read/write device variables and share a uniform view of constant memory.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) Memory Footprint
pages.summary: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) reserves device memory for parent-grid state synchronization and pending launch tracking, potentially requiring up to 860MB per parent generation.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) Nesting and Synchronization Depth
pages.summary: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) limits nesting depth to 24 and sets a default synchronization depth of 2, which can be configured via cudaDeviceSetLimit but is deprecated in CUDA 11.6+.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) Implicit (NULL) Stream
pages.summary: In CUDA Dynamic Parallelism version 1 (CDP1), the implicit NULL stream is shared between all threads in a block and does not insert implicit dependencies on pending work in other streams, unlike the host's NULL stream.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) Parameter Buffer Layout
pages.summary: Rules for laying out parameters in the parameter buffer for CDP1, including alignment requirements, prohibition of reordering, and a 4KB size limit.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) Pending Kernel Launches
pages.summary: Management of pending kernel launches in CUDA Dynamic Parallelism version 1 (CDP1) using a system-managed launch pool with fixed-size and virtualized components.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) Performance
pages.summary: Performance and synchronization considerations for CUDA Dynamic Parallelism version 1 are detailed in the CUDA C Programming Guide.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) Programming Guidelines
pages.summary: This page serves as a navigational reference for CUDA Dynamic Parallelism version 1 (CDP1) programming guidelines, directing users to the main documentation sections for basics and general guidelines.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) Device-side Launch from PTX
pages.summary: Low-level details for implementing dynamic parallelism in PTX using cudaLaunchDevice and cudaGetParameterBuffer.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) Implementation Restrictions
pages.summary: Hardware and software limitations in CDP1.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) Runtime
pages.summary: The CDP1 runtime section serves as a navigational pointer to the main Runtime documentation, directing users to the CDP2 version for detailed implementation specifics.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) Shared Memory
pages.summary: In CUDA Dynamic Parallelism version 1 (CDP1), shared memory can be declared statically or dynamically, but it cannot be passed directly to child kernels; data must be written back to global memory before launching children.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) SM Id and Warp Id
pages.summary: In CUDA Dynamic Parallelism 1 (CDP1), the PTX registers %smid and %warpid are volatile, meaning their values may change if the device runtime reschedules thread blocks, making it unsafe to rely on them remaining constant.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) Streams
pages.summary: Overview of stream handling in CUDA Dynamic Parallelism version 1 (CDP1), where streams are private to thread blocks, concurrency is not guaranteed, and explicit synchronization is deprecated.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) Symbol Addresses
pages.summary: In CUDA Dynamic Parallelism version 1, device symbols are accessed directly via the address-of operator, making runtime symbol APIs redundant and enforcing read-only access for constant symbols.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) Synchronization
pages.summary: Details on `cudaDeviceSynchronize` in CDP1, which synchronizes on all work launched by any thread in the thread-block but does not imply intra-block synchronization; explicit synchronization is deprecated in CUDA 11.6 and removed for compute_90+.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) Synchronization Performance
pages.summary: Explicit synchronization using cudaDeviceSynchronize() in device code is deprecated and inefficient; implicit synchronization at block end is preferred.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) Textures and Surfaces
pages.summary: In CUDA Dynamic Parallelism 1 (CDP1), dynamically created texture and surface objects can be passed to and used by child kernels, but their creation and destruction are restricted to the host, and legacy module-scope textures are not supported within device-launched kernels.

pages.title: CUDA Dynamic Parallelism (CDP1) Toolkit Support
pages.summary: This page covers the first-generation toolkit support for CUDA Dynamic Parallelism, including instructions for including the Device Runtime API in CUDA code.

pages.title: CUDA ECC Errors
pages.summary: ECC errors are not reported to kernel code but are detected by the host after launch completion, potentially generating exceptions.

pages.title: CUDA Environment Variables
pages.summary: Overview of CUDA environment variables used for configuring device enumeration, compilation, execution, module loading, and error logging.

pages.title: CUDA Error Log Management
pages.summary: CUDA Error Log Management provides mechanisms for activating, configuring, and retrieving error logs through specific APIs, while operating within defined limitations.

pages.title: CUDA Events
pages.summary: CUDA events provide a mechanism to monitor device progress and perform accurate timing by asynchronously recording checkpoints that complete when all preceding tasks or commands in a specified stream have finished.

pages.title: CUDA Extended Lambda ADL Lookup
pages.summary: The CUDA compiler's replacement of extended lambdas with placeholder types can inadvertently introduce additional namespaces into Argument Dependent Lookup (ADL) scopes, potentially causing ambiguous function resolution in host code.
aliases: CUDA Extended Lambda Argument Dependent Lookup

pages.title: CUDA Extended Lambda Capture Rules
pages.summary: Rules governing variable capture in CUDA extended lambdas, including value-only capture, array dimension limits, init-capture limitations, and handling of if-constexpr blocks.

pages.title: CUDA Extended Lambda Closure Layout
pages.summary: Captured variable expressions in extended lambdas passed to device code must remain unchanged regardless of `__CUDA_ARCH__` to ensure consistent closure class layout between host and device compilation.
aliases: CUDA Extended Lambda Closure Class Layout

pages.title: CUDA Extended Lambda Counter Stability
pages.summary: The CUDA compiler assigns a deterministic counter value to each extended lambda within a function for host compiler substitution, requiring that the definition order of these lambdas must not depend on the `__CUDA_ARCH__` macro.

pages.title: CUDA Extended Lambda Function Pointer Conversion
pages.summary: CUDA extended `__device__` lambdas can be converted to function pointers in device code, but this conversion is not supported in host code unless the lambda is also marked `__host__`.

pages.title: CUDA Extended Lambda MSVC Restrictions
pages.summary: When using Visual Studio host compilers, CUDA extended lambdas require the enclosing function to have external linkage and prohibit definition within `if-constexpr` blocks.
aliases: CUDA Extended Lambda Visual Studio Restrictions

pages.title: CUDA Extended Lambda Restrictions
pages.summary: Extended lambdas in CUDA C++ are subject to specific restrictions, including prohibitions on definition within local classes, usage in functions with deduced return types, and the inability to be generic lambdas.

pages.title: CUDA Extended Lambda Template Constraints
pages.summary: Rules governing template parameter definitions when an extended lambda is defined within a function or class template, including restrictions on variadic parameters, named parameters, and local/private types.

pages.title: CUDA Extended Lambda Type Introspection
pages.summary: Limitations on introspecting the return and parameter types of `__device__` extended lambdas in host code, and the role of the `__nv_is_extended_device_lambda_with_preserved_return_type` trait.
aliases: CUDA Extended Lambda Return Type Introspection

pages.title: CUDA Extended Lambda Type Traits Discrepancy
pages.summary: Discrepancies in standard C++ type traits (e.g., `std::is_trivially_copyable`) between the CUDA frontend and host compiler for extended lambda closure types, and warnings against using them in template instantiations.

pages.title: CUDA Extended Lambdas
pages.summary: CUDA extended lambdas allow explicit execution space annotations in lambda expressions, subject to strict nesting, enclosing function, and addressability constraints.

pages.title: CUDA External Resource Interoperability
pages.summary: CUDA External Resource Interoperability enables the import of external memory objects and synchronization semaphores from other APIs into the CUDA environment for device access and execution control.

pages.title: CUDA Family-Specific Features
pages.summary: CUDA family-specific features are architecture-specific capabilities common to multiple compute capabilities within a GPU family, introduced starting with Compute Capability 10.0, which require a family-specific compiler target to enable and restrict execution to members of that family.

pages.title: CUDA Feature Availability and Architecture-Specific Features
pages.summary: CUDA compute features are generally available on all subsequent architectures, but starting with Compute Capability 9.0, specialized architecture-specific features may be restricted to the exact compute capability they were compiled for.

pages.title: CUDA Feature Set Compiler Targets
pages.summary: Details on the three feature sets (Baseline, Architecture-Specific, Family-Specific) and their corresponding compiler targets (e.g., compute_100, compute_100a, compute_100f), including compatibility tables.

pages.title: CUDA Fire-and-Forget Stream
pages.summary: The CUDA fire-and-forget named stream (cudaStreamFireAndForget) allows launching work without stream tracking overhead or dependencies on previous grids, but cannot be used for events and requires 64-bit compilation.

pages.title: CUDA Floating-Point Standard
pages.summary: CUDA compute devices adhere to the IEEE 754-2008 standard for binary floating-point arithmetic with specific deviations in rounding, exception handling, NaN behavior, and atomic operations.

pages.title: __global__ Functions in CUDA
pages.summary: __global__ functions in CUDA are subject to specific restrictions regarding constexpr declarations, parameter types (excluding std::initializer_list, va_list, and rvalue references), variadic template pack placement, and the requirements for lambda closure types used as template arguments.

pages.title: cudaGraphInstantiateFlagAutoFreeOnLaunch
pages.summary: A flag for cudaGraphInstantiate that allows a graph to be relaunched while it still has unfreed memory allocations by automatically inserting an asynchronous free of these allocations at launch time.

pages.title: CUDA Graph Memory Remapping
pages.summary: CUDA Graph Memory Remapping occurs when graph execution contexts change, requiring physical memory reallocation to prevent data corruption, which incurs performance costs that can be avoided by consistent stream usage.

pages.title: CUDA Graph Memory Reuse
pages.summary: CUDA optimizes memory usage by reusing virtual addresses within a graph based on allocation lifetimes and reusing physical memory between non-concurrent graphs through virtual aliasing.

pages.title: CUDA Graph Peer Access
pages.summary: CUDA graph allocations can be configured for multi-GPU peer access, where the runtime maps virtual addresses to required peers, potentially granting more access than explicitly requested, though relying on such implicit mappings is an error.

pages.title: CUDA Graphs
pages.summary: CUDA Graphs present a model for work submission where a graph of operations is defined, instantiated, and then launched repeatedly, reducing CPU overhead and enabling optimizations.

pages.title: CUDA Heterogeneous Programming
pages.summary: CUDA heterogeneous programming models the GPU as a coprocessor with separate memory spaces from the CPU host, unified by Managed Memory.

pages.title: CUDA Host-Device Lambda Performance
pages.summary: Performance implications of `__host__ __device__` lambdas, specifically the inability of the host compiler to easily inline the lambda body due to indirect function calls.

pages.title: CUDA Implementation Restrictions
pages.summary: While Dynamic Parallelism guarantees all specified semantics, hardware and software resources are implementation-dependent and limit the scale, performance, and other properties of programs using the device runtime.

pages.title: CUDA Implicit (NULL) Stream
pages.summary: The CUDA device runtime provides a single implicit, unnamed stream shared by all threads in a block, which does not insert implicit dependencies on other streams due to the NonBlocking flag requirement.

pages.title: std::initializer_list in CUDA
pages.summary: In CUDA, std::initializer_list member functions are __host__ __device__ by default, allowing device-side usage, but the --no-host-device-initializer-list flag restricts them to __host__ only.

pages.title: Inline Namespaces in CUDA
pages.summary: The CUDA compiler injects references to device and global variables into host code, which can cause ambiguity errors if those variables are defined within inline namespaces that conflict with enclosing namespace definitions.

pages.title: Inline Unnamed Namespaces in CUDA
pages.summary: Entities such as __managed__, __device__, __shared__, __constant__ variables, __global__ functions, and surface/texture variables cannot be declared in namespace scope within an inline unnamed namespace.

pages.title: Intel Host Compiler Specifics in CUDA
pages.summary: When using the Intel compiler as a host compiler, nvcc enables the __INTEL_COMPILER_USE_INTRINSIC_PROTOTYPES macro to support Intel intrinsic functions in host code.

pages.title: CUDA Intrinsic Functions
pages.summary: CUDA intrinsic functions are faster, less accurate device-code-only alternatives to standard math functions, often enabled globally via the -use_fast_math compiler flag.

pages.title: CUDA Kernels
pages.summary: CUDA kernels are C++ functions defined with the __global__ specifier that execute in parallel across multiple CUDA threads, launched using the <<<...>>> execution configuration syntax.

pages.title: Lambda Expressions in CUDA
pages.summary: Lambda expressions in CUDA derive their execution space specifiers from the enclosing function scope, and their closure types are restricted in usage within __global__ function template arguments.

pages.title: CUDA C++ Language Restrictions
pages.summary: Overview of C++ language restrictions in CUDA device code, including host compiler extensions, __Complex types, __int128, and __float128 support conditions.

pages.title: CUDA Launch Setup APIs
pages.summary: Details the low-level device runtime APIs cudaGetParameterBuffer() and cudaLaunchDevice() used for kernel launches, including their C declarations, PTX mappings, and parameter buffer layout requirements.

pages.title: CUDA Lazy Loading
pages.summary: CUDA Lazy Loading is a feature that defers the loading of device code until it is actually needed, improving startup time and reducing memory usage, with specific version requirements for drivers, toolkits, and compilers.

pages.title: Likely/Unlikely Attributes in CUDA
pages.summary: The [[likely]] and [[unlikely]] standard attributes allow developers to hint to the CUDA device compiler optimizer about branch execution probability, though their use in host code without defined __CUDA_ARCH__ may trigger warnings on host compilers that do not support them.

pages.title: CUDA Linear Filtering
pages.summary: CUDA linear filtering is a mode available only for floating-point textures that computes interpolated values using weighted sums of neighboring texels based on fractional coordinates stored in a 9-bit fixed-point format.

pages.title: Long Double in CUDA
pages.summary: The long double data type is not supported in CUDA device code.

pages.title: __managed__ and __shared__ Variables in CUDA
pages.summary: __managed__ and __shared__ variables cannot be marked with the keyword constexpr.

pages.title: CUDA Mathematical Functions
pages.summary: Overview of mathematical functions supported in device code, including standard library functions and intrinsics, with notes on accuracy, error handling restrictions, and input validity.

pages.title: cudaMemcpyAsync Current Context/Device Sensitivity
pages.summary: cudaMemcpyAsync requires the calling thread's current context to match the stream's context when transferring memory allocated via cudaMallocAsync, whereas cudaMemcpyPeerAsync uses explicitly specified device primary contexts.

pages.title: CUDA Memory Allocation and Lifetime
pages.summary: Explains the distinct semantics of cudaMalloc/cudaFree between host and device, noting that device malloc maps to heap allocation and cross-environment free is an error.

pages.title: CUDA Memory Hierarchy
pages.summary: The CUDA memory hierarchy consists of private local memory, block-scoped shared memory, and globally accessible global, constant, and texture memory spaces, each optimized for different access patterns.

pages.title: CUDA Module
pages.summary: CUDA modules are dynamically loadable packages of device code and data, output by nvcc, that maintain symbol scope to allow interoperability between independent third-party modules within the same CUDA context.

pages.title: CUDA Nearest-Point Sampling
pages.summary: Nearest-point sampling is a texture filtering mode where the fetched value is determined by the floor of the texture coordinates, mapping to the nearest discrete texel.

pages.title: Noreturn Annotation in CUDA
pages.summary: The CUDA compiler (nvcc) supports noreturn annotations for both host and device functions, accommodating various host compiler syntaxes and the C++11 standard attribute.

pages.title: __nv_pure__ Attribute in CUDA
pages.summary: The __nv_pure__ attribute is supported for both host and device functions, translating to platform-specific attributes on the host and signaling immutability to the device optimizer.

pages.title: CUDA NVCC Compiler
pages.summary: nvcc is the compiler driver for CUDA that simplifies the compilation of C++ and PTX code into binary form for device execution.

pages.title: CUDA NVSCI Interoperability
pages.summary: Introduces NvSciBuf and NvSciSync interfaces for buffer allocation/exchange and synchronization management in CUDA interoperability.

pages.title: CUDA NVSCI Memory Import
pages.summary: This page describes the process of allocating NvSciBuf objects with specific CUDA-compatible attributes and importing them into CUDA as external memory.

pages.title: CUDA Occupancy Header (cuda_occupancy.h)
pages.summary: The cuda_occupancy.h header provides a standalone occupancy calculator and launch configurator implementation from Nsight Compute, useful for learning and environments where the full CUDA software stack is unavailable.

pages.title: CUDA OpenGL Interoperability
pages.summary: Overview of OpenGL-CUDA interoperability, including traditional interop and an alternative approach using Vulkan as an intermediary for memory and synchronization objects. Lists relevant OpenGL extensions (GL_EXT_memory_object, GL_EXT_semaphore, etc.).

pages.title: CUDA Overview
pages.summary: CUDA is a parallel computing platform and programming model developed by NVIDIA that enables GPU acceleration for compute-intensive applications using C, C++, and Fortran.

pages.title: CUDA Pending Kernel Launches
pages.summary: Details the fixed-size launch pool that tracks configuration and parameter data until kernel completion, which is configurable via cudaDeviceSetLimit.

pages.title: CUDA Performance Overhead
pages.summary: Describes the potential performance overhead imposed by system software tracking dynamic launches, which is incurred when linking against the device runtime library.

pages.title: cuda::pipeline Overview
pages.summary: The cuda::pipeline synchronization object manages and overlaps asynchronous data movement with computation using a double-ended N-stage queue that processes work in FIFO order.

pages.title: cuda::pipeline Interface Requirements
pages.summary: The cuda::pipeline interface requires CUDA 11.0 or later, ISO C++ 2011 compatibility, and the inclusion of the <cuda/pipeline> header, with a C-like alternative available for non-C++11 environments.

pages.title: Multi-Stage Asynchronous Data Copies using cuda::pipeline
pages.summary: The cuda::pipeline feature enables CUDA kernels to overlap memory transfers with computation by managing a sequence of asynchronous memcpy operations across multiple stages.

pages.title: Pipeline Primitives Interface
pages.summary: The Pipeline Primitives Interface provides a C-like API for managing memcpy_async operations, accessible via the <cuda_pipeline.h> header in ISO C++ 2011 compliant environments or <cuda_pipeline_primitives.h> otherwise.

pages.title: Single-Stage Asynchronous Data Copies using cuda::pipeline
pages.summary: Demonstrates using the cuda::pipeline API with a single stage to schedule asynchronous memory copies, allowing computation to overlap with data transfer.

pages.title: Specialized Producer/Consumer Roles in cuda::pipeline
pages.summary: The cuda::pipeline interface allows threads within a block to be assigned specific roles (producer, consumer, or both) to optimize memory transfer and computation overlap, with optimizations available for specific participation patterns.

pages.title: Unified Pipeline Loop Pattern
pages.summary: The Unified Pipeline Loop Pattern merges the prolog and epilog stages of a CUDA pipeline execution into a single nested loop structure, optimizing code conciseness while maintaining asynchronous memory transfers and computation overlap.

pages.title: CUDA Pointer Attributes
pages.summary: CUDA pointer attributes indicate how memory was allocated or registered rather than its physical location, allowing runtime detection of managed, host, device, or unregistered memory types via cudaPointerGetAttributes.

pages.title: CUDA Programming Guidelines Basics
pages.summary: The CUDA device runtime provides a functional subset of the host runtime API, allowing device code to perform device management, kernel launching, and stream operations using syntax largely identical to the host API.

pages.title: CUDA Programming Interface
pages.summary: The CUDA Programming Interface enables C++ developers to write device-executed programs through a minimal set of language extensions and a runtime library.

pages.title: CUDA Programming Model
pages.summary: The CUDA parallel programming model is a scalable architecture that leverages multicore CPUs and manycore GPUs through thread group hierarchies, shared memory, and barrier synchronization.

pages.title: CUDA Relaxed Constexpr
pages.summary: The -expt-relaxed-constexpr flag allows cross-execution space calls to constexpr functions in contexts requiring constant evaluation, relaxing default compiler restrictions.

pages.title: CUDA Runtime
pages.summary: The CUDA Runtime is implemented in the cudart library and provides the host-device memory model, asynchronous execution, error checking, and graphics interoperability APIs.

pages.title: CUDA Runtime Memory Footprint
pages.summary: The CUDA device runtime reserves memory for management tasks, specifically tracking pending grid launches, with configuration options available to reduce this footprint.

pages.title: Rvalue References in CUDA
pages.summary: By default, std::move and std::forward are considered __host__ __device__. The --no-host-device-move-forward flag disables this, making them __host__ only.

pages.title: CUDA Shared Memory
pages.summary: CUDA shared memory is a low-latency memory space enabling data sharing and cooperation among threads within a block, typically synchronized using the __syncthreads() barrier.

pages.title: CUDA Shared Memory on Device
pages.summary: Shared memory in CUDA device runtime can be declared statically or dynamically via extern variables, but cannot be passed directly to child kernels launched recursively.

pages.title: CUDA SLI Interoperability
pages.summary: CUDA SLI interoperability involves specific memory and context management considerations when integrating CUDA with Direct3D or OpenGL in multi-GPU SLI configurations.

pages.title: CUDA SM ID and Warp ID
pages.summary: The PTX instructions %smid and %warpid provide volatile identifiers for the Streaming Multiprocessor and warp, respectively, but their values may change due to device runtime rescheduling, making them unsafe to rely on across the lifetime of a thread or thread block.

pages.title: CUDA *this Capture Mode
pages.summary: Support for C++17 `*this` capture mode in CUDA lambdas, allowing capture of the object instance by value instead of the pointer, preventing runtime errors when accessing members on the GPU.

pages.title: CUDA Symbol Addresses on Device
pages.summary: Describes how __device__ symbols can be referenced directly via the & operator within kernels, noting that CUDA runtime APIs for symbol management are unsupported by the device runtime and __constant__ data is read-only.

pages.title: CUDA Table Lookup Using Textures
pages.summary: A method for implementing table lookups using texture filtering, specifically mapping an interval [0,R] to texture coordinates to ensure correct boundary values.

pages.title: CUDA Tail Launch Stream
pages.summary: The CUDA tail launch stream (cudaStreamTailLaunch) is a special stream that schedules a new grid for launch only after the parent grid and all its associated work have completed, acting as an implicit synchronization point.

pages.title: Templates in CUDA
pages.summary: CUDA restricts the use of certain types, such as those defined in host-only scopes, private class members, or unnamed types, as template arguments for __global__ function instantiations and __device__/__constant__ variable instantiations.

pages.title: CUDA Texture Fetching Coordinate Formulas
pages.summary: Formulas and rules for computing texture fetch values based on texture object attributes, including 1D, 2D, and 3D texture dimensions and coordinate normalization.

pages.title: CUDA Textures and Surfaces on Device
pages.summary: CUDA supports dynamically created texture and surface objects that can be created on the host and used on the device, but creation and destruction are restricted to the host side.

pages.title: CUDA Thread Block Clusters
pages.summary: Thread Block Clusters, introduced in Compute Capability 9.0, group up to eight thread blocks to guarantee co-scheduling on a single GPU Processing Cluster (GPC) and enable hardware-supported synchronization via Distributed Shared Memory.

pages.title: CUDA Thread Hierarchy
pages.summary: The CUDA thread hierarchy organizes threads into blocks and grids using 1D, 2D, or 3D indices to map parallel computations to data domains.

pages.title: Thread Local Storage in CUDA
pages.summary: The thread_local storage specifier is not allowed in device code.

pages.title: CUDA Time Function (clock/clock64)
pages.summary: The `clock()` and `clock64()` functions return a per-multiprocessor counter incremented every clock cycle, allowing developers to measure the total wall-clock time a thread spends executing on the device, including time-slicing overhead.

pages.title: CUDA Toolkit Compilation and Linking
pages.summary: Details the compilation and linking process for dynamic parallelism, including the need to link against libcudadevrt and examples using nvcc.

pages.title: CUDA Toolkit Device Runtime Inclusion
pages.summary: Prototypes for the CUDA device runtime API are included automatically during program compilation, eliminating the need for explicit inclusion of the cuda_device_runtime_api.h header.

pages.title: Trigraphs and Digraphs in CUDA
pages.summary: CUDA does not support trigraphs on any platform and does not support digraphs on Windows.

pages.title: CUDA User Objects
pages.summary: CUDA User Objects provide a reference-counted mechanism to manage the lifetime of resources associated with asynchronous work and CUDA Graphs, using destructor callbacks similar to C++ shared_ptr.

pages.title: Variable Templates in CUDA
pages.summary: CUDA supports variable templates for device and constant variables, though the Microsoft host compiler imposes restrictions on const qualification.

pages.title: CUDA Versioning and Compatibility
pages.summary: Explains the distinction between Compute Capability and the CUDA Driver API version (CUDA_VERSION), detailing the backward-compatible but not forward-compatible nature of the driver API and the requirements for installed drivers relative to applications and libraries.

pages.title: Virtual Base Classes in CUDA
pages.summary: CUDA prohibits passing objects of classes derived from virtual base classes as arguments to __global__ functions.

pages.title: Virtual Functions in CUDA
pages.summary: Virtual functions in CUDA require matching execution space specifiers for overrides, prohibit passing objects with virtual functions to global functions, and result in undefined behavior when invoking virtual functions across the execution space where the object was created.

pages.title: CUDA Vulkan Interoperability
pages.summary: CUDA Vulkan Interoperability allows applications to share memory and synchronization objects between CUDA and Vulkan by matching device UUIDs, importing memory via various handle types, and mapping buffers or mipmapped arrays onto the imported memory.

pages.title: Importing Vulkan Synchronization Objects into CUDA
pages.summary: Explains how to import Vulkan semaphore objects into CUDA using various handle types: opaque file descriptor (fd), NT handle, named NT handle, and D3DKMT handle.

pages.title: Signaling and Waiting on Imported Vulkan Synchronization Objects
pages.summary: Describes how to signal and wait on imported Vulkan semaphore objects from CUDA using `cudaSignalExternalSemaphoresAsync` and `cudaWaitExternalSemaphoresAsync`, emphasizing that the corresponding operations must be issued in Vulkan in the correct order.

pages.title: Windows-Specific Class Layout Constraints
pages.summary: When using the Microsoft host compiler, CUDA follows the IA64 ABI for class layout while MSVC does not, causing layout mismatches for types with virtual functions, virtual bases, or multiple inheritance that result in undefined behavior when passed between host and device code.

pages.title: cudaGetDriverEntryPointByVersion
pages.summary: The CUDA runtime API cudaGetDriverEntryPointByVersion allows users to request a specific CUDA driver version and follows guidelines similar to the driver entry point cuGetProcAddress.

pages.title: cudaGetDriverEntryPointByVersion
pages.summary: The CUDA runtime API `cudaGetDriverEntryPointByVersion` is designed to allow users to request a specific CUDA driver version. Unless specified otherwise, this function follows similar guidelines to the driver entry point `cuGetProcAddress`

pages.title: cudaMallocAsync and cudaFreeAsync
pages.summary: Core APIs for the Stream Ordered Memory Allocator that provide asynchronous memory management based on stream order, requiring explicit synchronization when sharing allocations across streams.

pages.title: cuGetProcAddress Error Handling
pages.summary: The `cuGetProcAddress` function can fail for two distinct reasons: API/usage errors and the inability to find the requested driver API symbol [CUDA_C_Programming_Guide:L20567-L20575].

pages.title: Guidelines for cuGetProcAddress
pages.summary: The `cuGetProcAddress` API provides a mechanism for loading CUDA runtime symbols dynamically. To ensure correct behavior and avoid errors or unexpected results, developers should adhere to the following guidelines when using this API.

pages.title: Data Migration and Coherency (Pre-6.x)
pages.summary: Pre-6.x GPUs require all managed memory to be transferred to GPU memory before kernel launch to avoid faults, whereas 6.x+ introduces on-demand page faulting for seamless Unified Memory.

pages.title: Data Prefetching in Unified Memory
pages.summary: Details the cudaMemPrefetchAsync API for migrating data closer to a specified processor asynchronously, including examples for System Allocator and Managed Memory prefetching patterns.

pages.title: Data Usage Hints (cudaMemAdvise)
pages.summary: Details the cudaMemAdvise API and its advice values: cudaMemAdviseSetReadMostly, cudaMemAdviseSetPreferredLocation, and cudaMemAdviseSetAccessedBy, including code examples and explanations of how these hints influence memory migration and access patterns.

pages.title: Default Memory Pools
pages.summary: The default memory pool is a non-migratable, device-local allocation pool retrieved via cudaDeviceGetDefaultMempool, whose accessibility can be modified via cudaMemPoolSetAccess but which does not support IPC.

pages.title: Default Stream
pages.summary: The default stream is the implicit stream (stream 0) used for kernel launches and memory operations that do not specify a stream parameter, with behavior varying based on the `--default-stream` compilation flag.

pages.title: Device Accessibility for Multi-GPU Support
pages.summary: Memory pool accessibility in CUDA is managed via cudaMemPoolSetAccess rather than peer access APIs, requiring devices to be peer-capable and applying settings globally to all pool allocations.

pages.title: Device Graph Creation
pages.summary: Device graph creation requires explicit instantiation using the cudaGraphInstantiateFlagDeviceLaunch flag, with structure fixed at instantiation time and performed exclusively on the host.

pages.title: Device Graph Launch Overview
pages.summary: Device graph launch enables dynamic control flow on the GPU by allowing graphs to be launched from the device, requiring unified addressing and prohibiting concurrent device-side launches of the same graph.

pages.title: Device Graph Requirements
pages.summary: This page details the constraints for launching graphs on a CUDA device, including single-device residency, allowed node types, restrictions on CUDA Dynamic Parallelism and MPS, and specific memory access rules for memcpy and memset operations.

pages.title: Device Graph Update
pages.summary: Covers the process of updating device graphs from the host and the requirement to re-upload the executable graph to the device for changes to take effect, noting undefined behavior if launched from the device during the update.

pages.title: Device Graph Upload
pages.summary: Device graph upload is the process of populating device resources before launching a CUDA graph, which can be done explicitly via cudaGraphUpload(), during instantiation via cudaGraphInstantiateWithParams(), or implicitly during the initial host launch.

pages.title: Device Launch Mechanics
pages.summary: Covers the mechanics of launching device graphs from host or device using cudaGraphLaunch(), noting that device graphs must be launched from another graph when launched from the device and that launch is per-thread.

pages.title: Device Launch Streams and Modes
pages.summary: Device graphs cannot be launched into regular CUDA streams and must instead use distinct named streams that denote specific launch modes: Fire and Forget, Tail Launch, and Sibling Launch.

pages.title: Device Memory
pages.summary: Device memory is the separate memory space on a GPU where kernels operate, allocated as linear memory (unified address space) or CUDA arrays (optimized for textures), with data transfer managed via specific runtime API functions.

pages.title: Device Memory Accesses
pages.summary: Device memory accesses are handled via transactions that may be re-issued based on address distribution within a warp, with global memory throughput heavily dependent on coalescing and alignment.

pages.title: Device Memory Space Specifiers
pages.summary: Restrictions on using __device__, __shared__, __managed__, and __constant__ specifiers on class members, parameters, and variable declarations, including rules for empty constructors/destructors and extern linkage in different compilation modes.

pages.title: Device Selection
pages.summary: The device a host thread operates on is set using `cudaSetDevice()`, which determines where memory allocations, kernel launches, streams, and events are associated.

pages.title: Diagnostic Pragmas
pages.summary: Diagnostic pragmas in CUDA control the severity of compiler messages and manage state, with modern usage requiring the `nv_` prefix.

pages.title: Direct Unified Memory Access from Host
pages.summary: Describes hardware support for coherent reads, stores, and atomic accesses from the host on GPU-resident unified memory, requiring specific device attributes and memory advice hints.

pages.title: Direct3D 9 Interoperability
pages.summary: Direct3D 9 interoperability allows CUDA kernels to map and modify Direct3D 9 vertex buffers, enabling dynamic vertex manipulation by integrating Direct3D device initialization with CUDA resource registration and mapping.

pages.title: Direct3D Interoperability
pages.summary: Overview of Direct3D 9Ex, 10, and 11 interoperability, device criteria, resource registration, and reference to specific version sections.

pages.title: Disabling Reuse Policies
pages.summary: Users may disable controllable memory reuse policies to avoid run-to-run variance or non-deterministic serialization, requiring explicit synchronization on allocation failure.

pages.title: Display Mode Switches and CUDA
pages.summary: Display mode switches alter the memory required for the GPU's primary surface, which can cannibalize CUDA memory allocations and cause subsequent CUDA runtime calls to fail with an invalid context error.

pages.title: Distributed Shared Memory
pages.summary: Distributed Shared Memory (DSM) is a feature introduced in compute capability 9.0 that allows threads in a thread block cluster to access the shared memory of all participating thread blocks within that cluster, providing a partitioned shared memory address space for read, write, and atomic operations.

pages.title: DPX (Data Parallel eXtensions)
pages.summary: DPX is a set of hardware-accelerated functions available on compute capability 9+ devices for performing min/max operations, fused addition/min/max, and optional ReLU clamping on integer parameters, primarily used in dynamic programming algorithms.

pages.title: Driver Entry Point Access
pages.summary: Driver Entry Point Access APIs allow retrieving the address of CUDA driver functions via function pointers, enabling ABI compatibility across different CUDA versions and access to new features with older toolkits.

pages.title: Dynamic Global Memory Allocation
pages.summary: Dynamic global memory allocation allows CUDA kernels to allocate and free memory from a fixed-size device heap using functions like malloc and free, with allocations persisting for the lifetime of the context.

pages.title: Dynamic Parallelism Device-Side Kernel Launch
pages.summary: Device-side kernel launch allows kernels to be launched from the device using the standard CUDA <<< >>> configuration syntax.

pages.title: Dynamic Parallelism Execution Model
pages.summary: The CUDA execution model extends to support dynamic parallelism by allowing device threads to configure, launch, and implicitly synchronize upon new grids, creating a nested parent-child hierarchy where the parent waits for all child grids to complete.

pages.title: Dynamic Parallelism Glossary
pages.summary: Definitions for key terms used in the Dynamic Parallelism section of the CUDA C Programming Guide, including Grid, Thread Block, Kernel Function, Host, Parent, Child, Thread Block Scope, and Device Runtime.

pages.title: Dynamic Parallelism Launch Asynchronicity
pages.summary: Device-side kernel launches are asynchronous, allowing the launching thread to continue execution until an implicit synchronization point is reached.

pages.title: Dynamic Parallelism Launch Environment Configuration
pages.summary: In dynamic parallelism, child kernels inherit global device configuration settings from the parent, while per-kernel configurations set from the host take precedence and cannot be overridden from the device.

pages.title: Dynamic Parallelism Memory Model
pages.summary: Parent and child grids share global and constant memory with weak consistency guarantees, while local and shared memory remain private and incoherent between execution contexts.

pages.title: Dynamic Parallelism Ordering and Concurrency
pages.summary: Kernel launch ordering follows CUDA Stream semantics, but no concurrency guarantees exist between different thread blocks or between parent and child grids.

pages.title: Dynamic Parallelism Programming Interface
pages.summary: The Device Runtime API provides a CUDA C++ interface for dynamic parallelism that mirrors the Host Runtime API, allowing per-thread kernel launches without inter-thread synchronization requirements.

pages.title: Dynamic Parallelism Streams and Events
pages.summary: Control of dependencies between grid launches using streams and events on the device, with strict scoping rules that define undefined behavior for cross-grid usage.

pages.title: Dynamic Parallelism Synchronization
pages.summary: Rules for synchronizing parent and child grids, including the deprecation of explicit `cudaDeviceSynchronize()` in device code and implicit synchronization upon grid exit.

pages.title: EGM Allocators
pages.summary: Extended GPU Memory (EGM) supports specific memory allocators that leverage NVLink routing for optimized access to system memory across NUMA nodes.

pages.title: EGM: Using CUDA Memory Pool
pages.summary: This page details the configuration and usage of CUDA Memory Pool APIs to create and manage Explicit Graph Memory (EGM) with peer access capabilities.

pages.title: Using the EGM Interface
pages.summary: This section covers the practical usage of the Extended GPU Memory (EGM) interface within the CUDA C programming model.

pages.title: EGM Memory Management APIs
pages.summary: Extensions to current CUDA APIs for memory management in EGM, including support for Virtual Memory and Stream Ordered Memory allocators with NUMA-aware location properties.

pages.title: EGM: Multi-Node, Single-GPU
pages.summary: This page details the configuration and memory allocation procedures for using the CUDA Extensible Graphics Memory (EGM) API in a multi-node, single-GPU environment, where memory is allocated on one node's NUMA domain and shared with another node via fabric handles.

pages.title: EGM Platforms and System Topology
pages.summary: Extended GPU Memory (EGM) supports three system topologies: Single-Node Single-GPU, Single-Node Multi-GPU, and Multi-Node Single-GPU, with specific device management requirements.

pages.title: EGM Preliminaries
pages.summary: An overview of the prerequisites for Extended GPU Memory (EGM), including supported topologies, identifier assignment, virtual memory management requirements, and relevant CUDA types.

pages.title: EGM: Single-Node, Multi-GPU
pages.summary: EGM in a single-node, multi-GPU configuration requires users to provide host placement information via NUMA node IDs, which can be retrieved using CUDA device attributes, before allocating memory through VMM or CUDA Memory Pool.

pages.title: EGM: Single-Node, Single-GPU
pages.summary: In a single-node, single-GPU configuration, existing CUDA host allocators and system-allocated memory can be utilized to leverage high-bandwidth CPU-to-CPU (C2C) communication, where local access behaves as it does for standard host allocations.

pages.title: EGM Socket Identifiers (NUMA)
pages.summary: EGM utilizes operating system-assigned NUMA node identifiers to manage memory placement, accessible via the CU_DEVICE_ATTRIBUTE_HOST_NUMA_ID device attribute.

pages.title: EGM: Using VMM APIs
pages.summary: This page details the usage of Virtual Memory Management (VMM) APIs for Explicit Global Memory (EGM) allocations, covering physical memory creation, address space reservation, mapping, and access protection.

pages.title: Error Checking
pages.summary: CUDA runtime functions return error codes for host-side validation, while asynchronous errors are managed via thread-local variables and synchronization.

pages.title: Error Log Management
pages.summary: Error Log Management is a legacy mechanism introduced in CUDA Toolkit 12.9 that reports CUDA API errors in plain English, replacing generic return codes with descriptive messages.

pages.title: Exception Handling
pages.summary: Exception handling is supported in host code but not in device code, and exception specifications are not supported for __global__ functions.

pages.title: exclusive_scan_update
pages.summary: exclusive_scan_update is a cooperative groups function that performs an exclusive scan over thread inputs while atomically updating a shared counter with the total sum, enabling dynamic buffer space allocation.

pages.title: Execution Configuration
pages.summary: The execution configuration defines the grid and block dimensions, optional shared memory allocation, and stream for a __global__ function call.

pages.title: Execution Environments
