pages.summary: Execution environments encapsulate device-side work for graphs and streams, providing a hierarchical model for synchronization where graph environments handle fire-and-forget work and stream environments parent graph launches.

pages.title: Explicit Memory Pools
pages.summary: Explicit memory pools allow applications to customize allocation properties such as IPC capability, maximum size, and NUMA node residency via the cudaMemPoolCreate API.

pages.title: Explicit Synchronization
pages.summary: Explicit synchronization in CUDA provides mechanisms for host threads and streams to coordinate execution states, including waiting for completion, querying status, and ordering operations via events.

pages.title: Explicit Synchronization and Logical GPU Activity
pages.summary: Explicit synchronization is required to ensure GPU completion because Unified Memory relies on logical activity, where kernels are not guaranteed to have finished until a host synchronization call is issued.

pages.title: Extended GPU Memory (EGM)
pages.summary: Extended GPU Memory (EGM) is a feature for integrated CPU-GPU NVIDIA systems that allows GPU threads to efficiently access all system memory, including CPU-attached memory and HBM3, via high-bandwidth interconnects like NVLink-C2C and NVSwitch.

pages.title: External Linkage
pages.summary: Rules for calling functions declared with extern qualifier from device code, requiring definition in the same compilation unit.

pages.title: Fabric Memory
pages.summary: Fabric Memory, introduced in CUDA 12.4, utilizes the CU_MEM_HANDLE_TYPE_FABRIC allocation handle to enable intra-node and inter-node memory sharing across Multi Node NVLINK systems, requiring device support verification and the NVIDIA IMEX daemon.

pages.title: File-backed Unified Memory
pages.summary: File-backed Unified Memory allows GPU kernels to directly access memory mapped from host files, though atomic operations are unsupported on systems lacking hostNativeAtomicSupported.

pages.title: Fire and Forget Launch
pages.summary: A fire-and-forget launch mode allows a graph to be submitted to the GPU immediately to run independently of the launching parent graph, with a limit of 120 such graphs per parent execution.

pages.title: Friend Functions
pages.summary: Restrictions on defining __global__ functions or function templates in friend declarations.

pages.title: Function Execution Space Specifiers
pages.summary: Function execution space specifiers (__global__, __device__, __host__) define where a function executes (host or device) and its callability, with __global__ and __device__ being mutually exclusive, while __host__ and __device__ can be combined.

pages.title: Function Inline Specifiers
pages.summary: Details on __noinline__, __forceinline__, and __inline_hint__ qualifiers for controlling compiler inlining behavior.

pages.title: Function Parameters
pages.summary: Restrictions on __global__ function parameters regarding size limits, varargs, and pass-by-reference, along with ODR-use requirements for separate compilation.

pages.title: Function Pointers
pages.summary: Restrictions on taking the address of __global__ or __device__ functions and using them across host/device boundaries.

pages.title: Function Recursion
pages.summary: __global__ functions in CUDA do not support recursion.

pages.title: __global__ Function Argument Processing
pages.summary: Details on how arguments are passed to __global__ functions, including memcpy vs copy constructor, and destructor timing issues due to asynchronous execution.

pages.title: Global Memory Alignment and Size Requirements
pages.summary: Global memory accesses must use data types of 1, 2, 4, 8, or 16 bytes that are naturally aligned to compile into single instructions for optimal coalescing; misaligned accesses result in multiple instructions and potential data corruption.

pages.title: GPU Architecture Overview
pages.summary: Overview of the NVIDIA GPU architecture, detailing the scalable array of Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs), SIMT execution model, hardware multithreading, and little-endian data representation.

pages.title: GPU Exclusive Access to Managed Memory (Pre-6.x)
pages.summary: On pre-6.x GPU architectures, the GPU holds exclusive access to managed memory while any kernel is active, requiring explicit CPU synchronization before any CPU access to managed data, even if the CPU and GPU are accessing different variables.

pages.title: GPU Memory Oversubscription with Unified Memory
pages.summary: Unified Memory allows applications to allocate and share arrays larger than the memory capacity of any individual processor, enabling out-of-core processing without significant complexity.

pages.title: GPU Memory Oversubscription (Pre-6.x)
pages.summary: Devices with compute capability lower than 6.0 are restricted from allocating more managed memory than the physical size of the GPU memory.

pages.title: Graph API Threading and Execution Constraints
pages.summary: This page details the threading safety rules for cudaGraph_t objects and the concurrency constraints for cudaGraphExec_t, clarifying that streams control ordering but not internal graph parallelism.

pages.title: Graph Memory Nodes
pages.summary: Graph Memory Nodes are a component of the CUDA Graph API, documented in Chapter 16 of the CUDA C Programming Guide.

pages.title: Graph Memory Nodes API Fundamentals
pages.summary: Graph memory nodes represent allocation and free actions within CUDA graphs, where allocations are GPU-ordered and their lifetimes are tied to execution flow rather than API calls.

pages.title: Graph Memory Nodes Introduction
pages.summary: Graph memory nodes enable graphs to create and own memory allocations with GPU-ordered lifetime semantics, allowing for fixed virtual addresses, driver-managed memory reuse, and physical memory aliasing across graph lifetimes.

pages.title: Graph Memory Nodes Support and Compatibility
pages.summary: Graph memory nodes require a CUDA driver version 11.4 or newer and GPU support for the stream ordered allocator, which can be verified using specific CUDA API calls.

pages.title: Graph Update
pages.summary: Graph update mechanisms allow modification of instantiated CUDA graphs without full re-instantiation, supporting both whole-graph and individual node updates under specific topological and parameter constraints.

pages.title: Graphics Interoperability
pages.summary: Graphics interoperability allows mapping OpenGL and Direct3D resources into the CUDA address space for data exchange, requiring explicit registration, mapping, and pointer access while preventing concurrent access.

pages.title: Grid Synchronization
pages.summary: Grid synchronization enables inter-thread block synchronization within a kernel using cooperative groups, requiring specific launch configurations and device support.

pages.title: Group Collectives
pages.summary: The Cooperative Groups library provides a set of collective operations that can be performed by a group of threads. These operations require participation of all threads in the specified group in order to complete the operation [CUDA_C_Prog

pages.title: Group Collectives
pages.summary: A set of collective operations provided by the Cooperative Groups library that require participation of all threads in a specified group, with strict requirements on argument consistency.

pages.title: 16-Bit Floating-Point Textures
pages.summary: Details on the 16-bit floating-point (half) format in CUDA, its IEEE 754-2008 binary2 compatibility, conversion intrinsics, promotion to 32-bit during fetch, and channel description creation.

pages.title: Hardware Multithreading and Resources
pages.summary: Hardware multithreading in CUDA maintains execution contexts on-chip to eliminate switching costs, with resource limits and occupancy determined by register and shared memory partitioning per multiprocessor.

pages.title: Hardware vs. Software Coherency in Unified Memory
pages.summary: This page compares hardware coherent systems, which use combined page tables and cache-line granularity to minimize TLB misses and contention, with software coherent systems that rely on separate page tables and page-fault-based migration.

pages.title: Host-Device Data Transfer Optimization
pages.summary: Techniques to minimize and optimize data transfers between host and device, including batching, using page-locked memory, mapped memory, and handling integrated systems.
aliases: Data Transfer between Host and Device

pages.title: Host Functions (Callbacks)
pages.summary: Host functions allow inserting CPU callbacks into a CUDA stream that execute after preceding commands complete, but must not make CUDA API calls to avoid deadlock.

pages.title: Host Native Atomics
pages.summary: Describes hardware-accelerated atomic accesses to CPU-resident memory on devices with hardware coherency, which eliminates the need for page fault emulation and is indicated by the cudaDevAttrHostNativeAtomicSupported attribute.

pages.title: Implicitly-declared and non-virtual explicitly-defaulted functions
pages.summary: Rules for determining execution space specifiers (__host__, __device__) for implicitly-declared or explicitly-defaulted functions based on callers and overridden virtual functions.

pages.title: Implicit Groups
pages.summary: Implicit groups represent the kernel's launch configuration (threads, blocks, grid) and serve as the starting point for group decomposition, requiring early, collective initialization to avoid deadlocks.

pages.title: Implicit Synchronization
pages.summary: Implicit synchronization occurs when operations from different streams are separated by an operation on the NULL stream, preventing concurrency unless non-blocking streams are used.

pages.title: Importing NvSciSync Objects
pages.summary: This page covers the process of creating NvSciSync attributes compatible with a CUDA device using `cudaDeviceGetNvSciSyncAttributes`, reconciling them, and importing the resulting NvSciSyncObj into CUDA as an external semaphore using `cudaImportExternalSemaphore`.

pages.title: Individual Node Enable/Disable
pages.summary: The CUDA Graph API allows specific kernel, memset, and memcpy nodes in an instantiated graph to be enabled or disabled using cudaGraphNodeSetEnabled(), where disabled nodes are functionally empty and parameter updates take effect upon re-enablement.

pages.title: Individual Node Update
pages.summary: Covers the mechanism for updating parameters of instantiated graph nodes (cudaGraphExec_t) directly, avoiding the overhead of re-instantiation.

pages.title: Inter-Process Communication (IPC) with Unified Memory
pages.summary: Discusses IPC capabilities for System-Allocated Memory on full support systems, noting that CUDA IPC does not support Managed Memory and highlighting techniques like mmap, POSIX IPC, and memfd_create.

pages.title: Interprocess Communication (IPC)
pages.summary: Interprocess Communication (IPC) allows sharing device memory pointers and event handles across different processes using the CUDA IPC API, supported on 64-bit Linux for compute capability 2.0+ devices, with specific limitations for managed memory and Tegra platforms.

pages.title: invoke_one
pages.summary: The `invoke_one` and `invoke_one_broadcast` collective functions in the CUDA cooperative groups library select a single arbitrary thread from a group to execute a function, with the latter distributing the result to all threads.

pages.title: IOMMU on Linux
pages.summary: CUDA on Linux bare-metal systems does not support IOMMU-enabled PCIe peer-to-peer memory copies, requiring users to disable IOMMU, whereas virtual machines can use IOMMU with VFIO pass-through.

pages.title: IPC Export Pool Limitations
pages.summary: IPC pools currently do not support releasing physical blocks back to the OS, causing cudaMemPoolTrimTo to act as a no-op and cudaMemPoolAttrReleaseThreshold to be ignored.

pages.title: IPC Import Pool Limitations
pages.summary: Import pools in CUDA cannot be used for new allocations or trimming, and their resource usage statistics only reflect imported allocations.

pages.title: IPC Memory Pools
pages.summary: IPC capable memory pools provide an easy, efficient, and secure mechanism for sharing GPU memory between processes, utilizing a two-phase approach that first establishes security and then coordinates virtual address mappings.

pages.title: Just-in-Time Compilation
pages.summary: Just-in-Time (JIT) compilation is the process where the CUDA device driver compiles PTX code to binary code at runtime, allowing applications to benefit from driver improvements and run on newer hardware, with the resulting binaries cached in the compute cache.

pages.title: Kernel Execution Parameters and Alignment
pages.summary: cuLaunchKernel passes kernel arguments via an array of pointers or a parameter buffer, where the latter requires strict alignment matching device code requirements to handle differences between host and device representations.

pages.title: L2 Cache Management
pages.summary: L2 Cache Management allows applications on CUDA compute capability 8.0+ devices to designate portions of the L2 cache for persisting data accesses, improving bandwidth and latency for repeated global memory accesses through set-aside sizing, access policy windows, and explicit reset mechanisms.

pages.title: labeled_partition
pages.summary: `labeled_partition` is a collective operation in the CUDA Cooperative Groups library that partitions a parent group into one-dimensional subgroups. Within these subgroups, threads are coalesced based on a provided label value.

pages.title: labeled_partition
pages.summary: A collective operation in the CUDA Cooperative Groups library that partitions a parent group into one-dimensional subgroups based on a label value, requiring Compute Capability 7.0.

pages.title: __launch_bounds__
pages.summary: The __launch_bounds__ qualifier allows developers to specify maximum threads per block, minimum blocks per multiprocessor, and maximum blocks per cluster to guide the compiler's register allocation and optimization heuristics.

pages.title: Layered Textures
pages.summary: Layered textures are one- or two-dimensional texture arrays supported on compute capability 2.0+ devices, created via cudaMalloc3DArray with the cudaArrayLayered flag and accessed using tex1DLayered or tex2DLayered functions.

pages.title: Lazy Loading (CUDA)
pages.summary: Lazy Loading is a CUDA feature that delays the loading of modules and kernels until they are actually used, reducing initialization time and memory overhead.

pages.title: Lazy Loading Issues: Allocators
pages.summary: Lazy loading delays kernel code loading to execution time, which can cause allocation failures if an application greedily reserves all VRAM at startup; solutions include using cudaMallocAsync(), adding buffer memory, or preloading kernels.

pages.title: Lazy Loading Issues: Autotuning
pages.summary: Lazy loading can skew benchmark results by including kernel load time, requiring warmup iterations or preloading to ensure accurate autotuning measurements.

pages.title: Lazy Loading Issues: Concurrent Execution
pages.summary: Lazy loading can cause deadlocks if context synchronization is required while kernels are executing concurrently, particularly in anti-patterns where one kernel waits for another.

pages.title: Querying Lazy Loading Status
pages.summary: Describes how to check if Lazy Loading is enabled using cuModuleGetLoadingMode() after initializing CUDA.

pages.title: Triggering Lazy Loading
pages.summary: Explains how kernels and variables are loaded automatically upon first use and provides methods to explicitly trigger loading without execution: cuModuleGetFunction() for Driver API and cudaFuncGetAttributes() for Runtime API.

pages.title: Lazy Loading Version Support
pages.summary: Lazy Loading requires CUDA 11.7+ Runtime and R515+ user-mode libraries, with no compiler support needed, and benefits are limited to libraries compiled with the newer toolkit.

pages.title: Link Compatibility across Toolkit Revisions
pages.summary: When linking device objects, if at least one object contains a kernel with a parameter larger than 4KB, all objects must be recompiled with the 12.1 toolkit or higher to avoid linker errors.

pages.title: Local Memory
pages.summary: Local memory is a device memory space used for automatic variables that cannot fit in registers, characterized by high latency and coalesced access patterns.

pages.title: Managed Memory (CUDA)
pages.summary: CUDA Managed Memory uses cudaMallocManaged to provide unified memory access, with behavior varying based on hardware coherency support (directManagedMemAccessFromHost) regarding page faults and data migration between CPU and GPU.

pages.title: Managed Memory on Devices with Compute Capability 6.x+
pages.summary: CUDA Managed Memory is fully supported and coherent on devices with compute capability 6.x or higher without pageable memory access, though system allocators cannot be used.

pages.title: __managed__ Memory Space Specifier
pages.summary: The __managed__ memory space specifier allows variables to be accessed by both host and device code, subjecting them to specific restrictions regarding constancy, references, initialization contexts, and multi-GPU allocation.

pages.title: Managed Memory on Windows or Compute Capability 5.x
pages.summary: Describes CUDA Managed Memory v1.0 support on devices with compute capability lower than 6.0 or Windows platforms, highlighting limited support for data migration, coherency, and oversubscription.

pages.title: __managed__ Variables
pages.summary: Global-scope managed variables using __managed__ keyword behave like cudaMallocManaged, simplifying data exchange between host and device by eliminating manual memory allocation and copying.

pages.title: Mapping Buffers onto Imported Memory Objects
pages.summary: Describes how to map a device pointer onto an imported memory object (NvSciBufObj) using `cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedBuffer`, specifying offset and size, and notes that the resulting pointer must be freed using `cudaFree()`.

pages.title: Mapping Memory
pages.summary: The cuMemMap function associates physical memory allocations with reserved virtual address ranges, enabling users to map, unmap, and remap memory within the CUDA Virtual Memory Management API.

pages.title: Mapping Mipmapped Arrays onto Imported Memory Objects
pages.summary: Describes how to map a CUDA mipmapped array onto an imported memory object using `cudaExternalMemoryGetMappedMipmappedArray` with parameters defined in `cudaExternalMemoryMipmappedArrayDesc`, noting that the number of mip levels must be 1.

pages.title: Maximize Instruction Throughput
pages.summary: This section refers readers to the CUDA C++ Best Practices Guide for detailed strategies on optimizing instruction throughput.

pages.title: Maximize Memory Throughput
pages.summary: Strategies for maximizing memory throughput include minimizing low-bandwidth host-device transfers, maximizing on-chip memory usage, and optimizing global memory access patterns.

pages.title: Maximizing Utilization
pages.summary: Strategies to maximize GPU utilization by exposing parallelism across application, device, and multiprocessor levels through asynchronous execution, latency hiding via warp residency, and resource optimization.

pages.title: __maxnreg__
pages.summary: The __maxnreg__ qualifier is a CUDA C++ function attribute used to specify the maximum number of registers to be allocated to a single thread, serving as a low-level performance tuning mechanism.

pages.title: memcpy_async
pages.summary: `memcpy_async` is a group-wide collective function that utilizes hardware-accelerated support for nonblocking memory transactions from global to shared memory [CUDA_C_Programming_Guide:L12632-L12677]. It is part of the `cooperative_groups`

pages.title: memcpy_async Performance Guidance
pages.summary: Performance guidance for `memcpy_async` is primarily relevant for devices with compute capability 8.x, where the pipeline mechanism is shared among CUDA threads within the same warp. This sharing causes batches of `memcpy_async` to be entan

pages.title: Memcpy/Memset Behavior with Stream-Associated Unified Memory
pages.summary: Details the rules for cudaMemcpy* and cudaMemset* operations on devices where concurrentManagedAccess is not set, explaining how coherency in the copy stream determines whether data is accessed from host or device, and the implications for CPU access during these operations.

pages.title: Memcpy/Memset Behavior with Unified Memory
pages.summary: Explains how cudaMemcpy* and cudaMemset* functions interact with unified memory pointers, providing performance advice on using accurate cudaMemcpyKind hints, preferring cudaMemcpyDefault, avoiding initialization via these APIs, and using kernels or std::memcpy for system-allocated memory.

pages.title: memcpy_async
pages.summary: A group-wide collective API that utilizes hardware-accelerated, nonblocking memory transactions to copy data from global to shared memory, requiring 16-byte alignment and specific Compute Capability levels for asynchronous execution.

pages.title: memcpy_async Performance Guidance
pages.summary: Performance considerations for memcpy_async on compute capability 8.x, covering alignment requirements, trivially copyable type constraints, and warp entanglement effects on pipeline operations.

pages.title: Memory Barrier Primitives (__mbarrier)
pages.summary: C-like interfaces to cuda::barrier functionality available via <cuda_awbarrier_primitives.h>, providing data types and API functions for managing shared memory synchronization phases.

pages.title: Memory Discarding in Unified Memory
pages.summary: The `cudaMemDiscardBatchAsync` and `cudaMemDiscardAndPrefetchBatchAsync` APIs allow applications to inform the CUDA runtime that memory contents are no longer useful, enabling the driver to skip redundant migrations and optimize performance.

pages.title: Memory Fence Functions
pages.summary: Memory fence functions in CUDA enforce sequentially-consistent ordering on memory accesses across different thread scopes to prevent data races in the weakly-ordered memory model.

pages.title: Memory Fences and Synchronization in CUDA
pages.summary: Memory fences and synchronization functions in CUDA are used to ensure the ordering and visibility of memory operations across threads and blocks, enabling safe coordination such as atomic counter-based completion signaling.

pages.title: Memory Paging and Page Sizes in Unified Memory
pages.summary: This page defines virtual and physical pages, page sizes, page tables, and TLBs, explaining how virtual addressing and page size choices impact performance in Unified Memory systems.

pages.title: Memory Pools
pages.summary: Memory pools encapsulate virtual and physical memory resources, allowing allocations to be managed according to specific pool attributes and properties via APIs like cudaMallocAsync and cudaDeviceSetMempool.

pages.title: Memory Reuse Policies
pages.summary: The CUDA stream ordered allocator attempts to reuse memory freed via cudaFreeAsync() before requesting new memory from the OS, with behavior controlled by specific pool attributes.

pages.title: Memory Synchronization Domains
pages.summary: Memory Synchronization Domains, introduced in Hopper architecture (CUDA 12.0), alleviate memory fence interference by isolating traffic into distinct domains, allowing the GPU to reduce the 'net cast' of waiting operations.

pages.title: Memory Type
pages.summary: Introduction to CUDA 10.2+ memory type allocation via cuMemCreate and CUmemAllocationProp::allocFlags, allowing applications to specify memory features and ensuring device support.

pages.title: Minimize Memory Thrashing
pages.summary: Strategies to avoid performance degradation from frequent memory allocations and frees, including sizing allocations appropriately, batching operations, and using cudaMallocManaged for oversubscription support.

pages.title: Modular Programs and Data Access Constraints
pages.summary: Discusses the use of cudaMemAttachHost flag with cudaMallocManaged to create allocations initially invisible to device execution, ensuring safe independence between threads in modular programs, and mentions alternative barrier-based approaches.

pages.title: Multi-Device Systems
pages.summary: Overview of managing multiple CUDA-enabled devices on a host system, including enumeration, selection, and inter-device communication.

pages.title: Multi-GPU Managed Memory (Pre-6.x)
pages.summary: Details how managed allocations are visible to all GPUs via peer-to-peer on pre-6.x systems, describes migration to system memory if peer-to-peer support is missing, and outlines Windows-specific behaviors involving zero-copy fallback and environment variables.

pages.title: Multicast Support
pages.summary: Multicast Object Management APIs allow applications to leverage NVLINK SHARP for broadcast and reduction operations by creating Multicast Objects, forming Multicast Teams across NVSWITCH-connected GPUs, and using multimem PTX instructions.

pages.title: Multicast Support Query
pages.summary: Applications must query device support for Multicast Objects using CU_DEVICE_ATTRIBUTE_MULTICAST_SUPPORTED before attempting to use them.

pages.title: Namespace Reservations
pages.summary: It is undefined behavior to add declarations or definitions to the cuda::, nv::, or cooperative_groups:: namespaces or any namespaces nested within them.

pages.title: Nanosleep Function (__nanosleep)
pages.summary: The __nanosleep function suspends a thread for a specified duration in nanoseconds, with a maximum sleep time of approximately 1 millisecond, and is supported on devices with compute capability 7.0 or higher.

pages.title: #pragma nv_abi
pages.summary: The #pragma nv_abi directive allows applications compiled in separate compilation mode to achieve performance similar to whole program compilation by specifying custom Application Binary Interface (ABI) properties.

pages.title: Occupancy-Based Kernel Launch
pages.summary: Demonstrates using the CUDA occupancy API (cudaOccupancyMaxPotentialBlockSize) to automatically determine optimal block size and grid size for a kernel launch based on device capabilities and kernel resource usage.

pages.title: Occupancy Calculator API
pages.summary: The Occupancy Calculator API provides functions to predict and optimize GPU kernel execution configurations based on thread block size, cluster size, and shared memory usage.

pages.title: OpenGL Interoperability
pages.summary: Details on interoperating with OpenGL buffers, textures, and renderbuffers, including registration functions, usage hints, bindless texture limitations, and a code sample for modifying vertex buffers.

pages.title: Operator Function
pages.summary: An operator function in CUDA cannot be declared as a __global__ function.

pages.title: Operators
pages.summary: Restrictions on assignment and address operators for __constant__, __shared__, and built-in variables.

pages.title: Page-Locked Host Memory
pages.summary: Page-locked (pinned) host memory is a type of host memory allocation that enables concurrent data transfers, zero-copy access, and higher bandwidth on certain systems, allocated via CUDA runtime functions like `cudaHostAlloc()` or `cudaHostRegister()`.

pages.title: Peer-to-Peer Memory Access
pages.summary: Peer-to-Peer (P2P) memory access allows kernels on one GPU to directly dereference pointers to memory allocated on another GPU, provided the system topology supports it and peer access is explicitly enabled.

pages.title: Peer-to-Peer Memory Copy
pages.summary: Peer-to-Peer (P2P) memory copy allows data transfer between different GPU devices using specific CUDA API functions or unified address space, offering synchronization guarantees and performance benefits when peer access is enabled.

pages.title: Performance Optimization Strategies
pages.summary: Performance optimization in CUDA revolves around four basic strategies: maximizing parallel execution, optimizing memory and instruction usage, and minimizing memory thrashing, with efforts guided by profiling tools.

pages.title: Physical Page Caching Behavior
pages.summary: The CUDA allocator minimizes OS calls by caching physical pages within a pool, controlled by the release threshold attribute, and allows explicit shrinking via cudaMemPoolTrimTo.

pages.title: Pointer Attributes
pages.summary: The cuPointerGetAttributes function supports querying attributes for stream ordered allocations, including device ordinal and memory pool handle.

pages.title: Pointers
pages.summary: Rules regarding pointer dereferencing across host/device boundaries and usage of addresses obtained from __device__, __shared__, or __constant__ variables.

pages.title: #pragma unroll
pages.summary: A compiler directive used to control the unrolling of loops in CUDA C/C++ code.

pages.title: Formatted Output (printf) in CUDA
pages.summary: CUDA provides device-side printf() support for compute capability 2.x and higher, allowing kernels to send formatted strings to a host-side buffer that is flushed via synchronization or memory operations.

pages.title: Profiler Counter Function
pages.summary: The __prof_trigger() function allows applications to increment per-multiprocessor hardware counters (0-7) for monitoring via nvprof.

pages.title: Programmatic Dependent Launch
pages.summary: A mechanism allowing a secondary kernel to launch before its primary dependency completes, enabling concurrent execution of independent work.

pages.title: PTX Compatibility
pages.summary: PTX compatibility is determined by the -arch compiler option, with architecture-specific targets being incompatible across generations and family-specific targets offering forward compatibility within a family.

pages.title: PTX Device-Side Launch
pages.summary: Low-level PTX support for dynamic parallelism kernel launches via specific APIs.

pages.title: Querying Data Usage Attributes on Managed Memory
pages.summary: Describes how to query memory range attributes set via cudaMemAdvise or cudaMemPrefetchAsync using the cudaMemRangeGetAttribute API, covering attributes for read-mostly hints, preferred locations, access history, and prefetch history.

pages.title: Read/Write Coherency
pages.summary: Texture and surface memory are cached and not kept coherent with global memory writes within the same kernel call, meaning reads to addresses written to in the same kernel return undefined data.

pages.title: reduce
pages.summary: Performs a reduction operation on data provided by each thread in a cooperative group, supporting hardware acceleration on Compute Capability 8.0+ for specific operators and providing asynchronous variants for atomic updates or pointer stores.

pages.title: Reduce Operators
pages.summary: Reduce operators are function objects provided by the `cooperative_groups` (cg) namespace to facilitate reduction operations within cooperative groups. These operators are designed to mirror the behavior of hardware intrinsics available on

pages.title: Reduce Operators
pages.summary: Function objects in the cooperative_groups namespace that mirror hardware intrinsics for basic reduction operations like addition, comparison, and bitwise logic, differing from STL by returning values rather than booleans.

pages.title: Reserving a Virtual Address Range
pages.summary: Applications must reserve a virtual address range using cuMemAddressReserve to hold memory allocations; this range is initially unmapped, can be mapped to memory from any device, and must be returned using cuMemAddressFree after unmapping.

pages.title: Resource Usage Statistics
pages.summary: Introduced in CUDA 11.3, resource usage statistics provide attributes to query current and high-watermark values for reserved and used memory within a CUDA memory pool.

pages.title: Restricted Pointers (__restrict__)
pages.summary: The __restrict__ keyword allows programmers to assert that pointers do not alias, enabling compiler optimizations like common sub-expression elimination and code reordering, though it may increase register pressure and negatively impact occupancy in CUDA.

pages.title: cudaMemPoolReuseAllowInternalDependencies
pages.summary: This memory pool reuse policy allows the CUDA driver to insert dependencies into the allocating stream to reuse memory whose availability depends on another stream's progress when physical allocation fails.

pages.title: cudaMemPoolReuseAllowOpportunistic
pages.summary: This policy enables the CUDA memory allocator to reuse freed memory based on stream progress semantics without requiring explicit event dependencies.

pages.title: cudaMemPoolReuseFollowEventDependencies
pages.summary: This memory pool policy allows the allocator to reuse memory freed in one stream for allocations in another stream by examining dependencies established by CUDA events.

pages.title: Run Time Type Information (RTTI)
pages.summary: Run Time Type Information (RTTI) features such as typeid, std::type_info, and dynamic_cast are supported in host code but are not available in device code within CUDA.

pages.title: Runtime and Driver API Interoperability
pages.summary: CUDA applications can mix Runtime and Driver API code, allowing contexts and device memory to be shared between the two interfaces through implicit context management and pointer casting.

pages.title: Runtime Initialization
pages.summary: As of CUDA 12.0, cudaInitDevice() and cudaSetDevice() explicitly initialize the CUDA runtime and primary context, triggering JIT compilation and requiring error checking, while implicit initialization occurs on device 0 if no explicit call is made.

pages.title: inclusive_scan and exclusive_scan
pages.summary: Performs inclusive and exclusive scan operations on data provided by threads within a cooperative group, with variants for atomic updates.

pages.title: Shared Memory
pages.summary: Shared memory is a fast, software-managed scratchpad memory allocated with the __shared__ specifier that minimizes global memory accesses by allowing threads within a block to cooperate and reuse data.

pages.title: Shared Memory Banks and Conflicts
pages.summary: Shared memory is divided into banks to allow simultaneous access for high bandwidth, but accessing multiple addresses within the same bank causes bank conflicts that serialize access and reduce throughput.

pages.title: Sibling Launch
pages.summary: Sibling launch is a variation of fire-and-forget launch where the graph is launched as a child of the launching graph's parent environment, ensuring it does not gate tail launches of the parent.

pages.title: Signaling and Waiting on Imported Synchronization Objects
pages.summary: Describes how to signal and wait on imported NvSciSync-backed semaphore objects using `cudaSignalExternalSemaphoresAsync` and `cudaWaitExternalSemaphoresAsync`, including the use of flags to skip memory synchronization when cache coherency is not required.

pages.title: SIMD Video Instructions
pages.summary: PTX ISA v3.0+ SIMD video instructions for 16-bit and 8-bit values.

pages.title: SIMT Architecture and Warps
pages.summary: SIMT (Single Instruction, Multiple Threads) architecture executes threads in groups of 32 called warps, where threads share an instruction stream but maintain independent state, with Volta architecture introducing Independent Thread Scheduling to allow per-thread concurrency and sub-warp divergence.

pages.title: Standard Library
pages.summary: Standard libraries are only supported in host code, but not in device code, unless specified otherwise.

pages.title: Static Variables within Function
pages.summary: Rules governing the declaration, memory space specifiers, and initialization restrictions for static variables declared within function scope in CUDA.

pages.title: Stream Association Examples
pages.summary: Demonstrates the use of cudaStreamAttachMemAsync() to control CPU/GPU concurrency by associating managed memory with specific streams or the host, and highlights the consequences of improper association.

pages.title: Managing Data Visibility with cudaStreamAttachMemAsync
pages.summary: cudaStreamAttachMemAsync() allows finer-grained control over managed memory visibility by associating allocations with specific streams, enabling CPU access once that stream's operations complete even if other streams are active.

pages.title: Stream Attach with Multithreaded Host Programs
pages.summary: Explains the primary use of cudaStreamAttachMemAsync() in multithreaded programs to enable independent task parallelism by associating CPU thread allocations with private streams.

pages.title: Stream Capture Graph Memory
pages.summary: Graph memory nodes are created by capturing stream-ordered allocation and free calls (cudaMallocAsync and cudaFreeAsync), allowing the returned virtual addresses to be used by other operations within the graph while ensuring proper ordering via captured stream dependencies.

pages.title: Stream and Event Behavior in Multi-Device Systems
pages.summary: Details on how streams and events behave across multiple devices, including failure conditions for kernel launches and event operations, success conditions for memory copies and synchronization, and the independence of default streams.

pages.title: Stream Ordered Memory Allocator
pages.summary: A legacy allocator (CUDA 11.3+) that enables ordering memory allocation/deallocation with stream work (cudaMallocAsync, cudaFreeAsync), improving memory reuse and reducing OS calls, but replaced by newer guides in CUDA 13.0.

pages.title: Stream Ordered Memory Allocator Query
pages.summary: Support for the Stream Ordered Memory Allocator is queried via cudaDevAttrMemoryPoolsSupported, while IPC support is queried via cudaDevAttrMemoryPoolSupportedHandleTypes, requiring driver version checks to avoid errors on older drivers.

pages.title: Stream Overlap
pages.summary: The amount of execution overlap between streams depends on device capabilities (concurrent data transfers, kernel execution overlap) and the order of commands issued.
aliases: Overlap of Data Transfer and Kernel Execution

pages.title: Stream Priorities
pages.summary: Stream priorities allow influencing task execution order via `cudaStreamCreateWithPriority`, serving as hints for the GPU scheduler rather than strict guarantees.

pages.title: Streams
pages.summary: Streams are ordered sequences of commands that enable concurrent execution across multiple streams, managed via creation and destruction APIs with automatic resource cleanup.

pages.title: Surface Memory
pages.summary: Surface memory allows read and write access to CUDA arrays via surface objects on devices with compute capability 2.0 and higher.

pages.title: Surface Object API
pages.summary: The Surface Object API allows creating surface objects from CUDA arrays using cudaCreateSurfaceObject(), utilizing byte addressing for read/write operations distinct from texture memory.

pages.title: sync
pages.summary: Synchronizes the threads named in the group, equivalent to T.barrier_wait(T.barrier_arrive()).

pages.title: Synchronization API Actions
pages.summary: Synchronization API actions optimize memory management by integrating the allocator with the CUDA driver, allowing the driver to guarantee completion of specific frees and release excess physical memory based on the release threshold during synchronization.

pages.title: Synchronous Calls
pages.summary: Synchronous functions block the host thread until the device completes the requested task, with post-completion behavior (yield, block, or spin) configurable via cudaSetDeviceFlags().

pages.title: System-Allocated Memory
pages.summary: System-allocated memory refers to standard system memory (malloc, mmap, new) that is treated as unified memory on supported systems, utilizing first-touch policy for physical allocation and supporting CUDA Unified Memory APIs.

pages.title: System-Allocated Memory: In-depth Examples
pages.summary: Demonstrates advanced usage patterns for system-allocated memory in CUDA Unified Memory, including host malloc, managed memory, stack variables, and global/extern variables, while highlighting the requirement to pass pointers for non-managed global variables.

pages.title: System Allocator (CUDA)
pages.summary: Demonstrates the use of system-allocated memory (malloc) in CUDA, highlighting the need for explicit memory advice (cudaMemAdvise) and the impact of hardware coherency (directManagedMemAccessFromHost) on CPU/GPU memory migration behavior.

pages.title: Tail Launch
pages.summary: Tail Launch is a device-side graph execution mode that enables serial work dependencies by launching subsequent graphs only after a parent graph and all its children have completed.

pages.title: Tail Self-Launch
pages.summary: Tail self-launch allows a device graph to enqueue itself for execution, facilitated by the `cudaGetCurrentGraphExec()` function which queries the handle of the currently running graph.

pages.title: Tesla Compute Cluster (TCC) Mode
pages.summary: TCC mode is a configuration for Tesla and Quadro Series devices on Windows that removes graphics functionality to optimize the device for compute tasks.

pages.title: Tensor Map
pages.summary: A tensor map is a hardware object describing the layout of a multi-dimensional array in global and shared memory, required for bulk tensor asynchronous copies and typically created via cuTensorMapEncode.

pages.title: Tensor Map Creation and Host-to-Device Transfer
pages.summary: This page details the creation of tensor maps using the cuTensorMapEncodeTiled driver API and describes three methods for transferring them to the device: as __grid_constant__ kernel parameters, via __constant__ memory, or via global memory with explicit fencing.

pages.title: Tensor Memory Accelerator (TMA)
pages.summary: The Tensor Memory Accelerator (TMA) is a hardware feature introduced in Compute Capability 9.0 that provides an efficient data transfer mechanism from global memory to shared memory for multi-dimensional arrays, offloading address calculations and repetitive loading/storing operations.

pages.title: Texture Gather
pages.summary: Texture gather is a special 2D texture fetch operation performed by tex2Dgather() that returns four 32-bit values corresponding to a specific component of the four texels used for bilinear filtering, subject to 8-bit fractional precision limits and specific hardware and memory requirements.

pages.title: Texture Memory
pages.summary: Texture memory is a read-only memory space in CUDA kernels accessed via texture fetches, configured through texture objects that define dimensionality, texel types, read modes, coordinate normalization, addressing modes, and filtering modes.

pages.title: Texture Object API
pages.summary: The Texture Object API provides a mechanism to create and manage texture objects in CUDA using resource and texture descriptions, enabling efficient texture sampling within kernels.

pages.title: Texture and Surface Memory
pages.summary: Texture and surface memory provide cached access to device memory, optimized for 2D spatial locality and offering performance benefits such as offloaded addressing calculations and data conversion.

pages.title: TMA One-Dimensional Operations
pages.summary: Procedures for using TMA to transfer one-dimensional contiguous arrays, including initializing shared memory barriers, initiating bulk-asynchronous copies (Global to Shared and Shared to Global), synchronizing threads, and handling alignment requirements.

pages.title: TMA Multi-Dimensional (2D) Operations
pages.summary: Implementation of Tensor Memory Accelerator (TMA) for 2D array transfers, enabling asynchronous bulk copies between global and shared memory using specific PTX/CUDA functions and requiring 128-byte alignment.

pages.title: TMA Alignment and Bounds Handling
pages.summary: Rules for out-of-bounds handling (zero-filling shared memory for reads, no negative indices for writes) and alignment/stride requirements for global and shared memory in Compute Capability 9.0 TMA operations.

pages.title: TMA Completion Mechanisms
pages.summary: Tensor Memory Accelerator (TMA) data transfers are asynchronous, utilizing Shared Memory Barriers (mbarrier) for Global-to-Shared reads and Bulk Async-Group mechanisms for Shared-to-Global/Shared writes to signal completion.

pages.title: Device-side Tensor Map Encoding
pages.summary: Process for encoding a tiled-type tensor map on the device using a host-created template, modifying it in shared memory, and copying it back to global memory.

pages.title: TMA Tensor Map Release-Acquire Pattern
pages.summary: Synchronization requirements for using a modified tensor map in global memory, involving `tensormap_cp_fenceproxy` (release) and `fence_proxy_tensormap_generic` (acquire) to ensure visibility and ordering between threads.

pages.title: TMA Swizzle for Shared Memory Bank Conflict Avoidance
pages.summary: TMA swizzle patterns (None, 32B, 64B, 128B) rearrange data in shared memory during Tensor Memory Accelerator (TMA) transfers to eliminate bank conflicts, requiring specific alignment and access patterns.

pages.title: TMA Swizzle Modes and Requirements
pages.summary: TMA swizzle patterns map 16-byte chunks of global memory to shared memory banks to avoid bank conflicts, requiring specific alignment and inner dimension constraints based on the swizzle width.

pages.title: Toolkit and Driver Compatibility
pages.summary: CUDA 12.1 Toolkit and r530 driver or higher are required to support kernel parameters larger than 4KB.

pages.title: Trap Function
pages.summary: The __trap() function initiates a trap operation from any device thread, aborting kernel execution and raising an interrupt in the host program.

pages.title: Undefined Behavior in Cross-Execution Space Calls
pages.summary: Defines undefined behavior scenarios for calls between __host__ and __device__ functions based on __CUDA_ARCH__ definition.

pages.title: Unified Memory Programming
pages.summary: Unified Memory Programming provides a single, system-wide address space for CPU and GPU memory, managed via APIs like cudaMallocManaged and __managed__ variables, with support levels varying by device architecture.

pages.title: Unified Memory Atomics and Synchronization
pages.summary: Covers CUDA Unified Memory support for atomic operations and heterogeneous synchronization primitives (cuda::atomic, cuda::barrier, etc.), detailing behavior on systems with and without CPU/GPU page tables.

pages.title: Unified Memory Introduction
pages.summary: Unified Memory is a programming model introduced in CUDA 7.0 that provides a single, unified memory pool accessible by both CPU and GPU, with support varying based on device compute capability.

pages.title: Unified Memory on Devices Without Full Support
pages.summary: This section details the limitations and fallback mechanisms for Unified Memory on devices that do not fully support CUDA Unified Memory, specifically those limited to CUDA Managed Memory.

pages.title: Unified Memory on Devices with Full CUDA Unified Memory Support
pages.summary: This section covers systems with full CUDA Unified Memory support, where the device can access any memory owned by the host process.

pages.title: Unified Memory Performance Hints
pages.summary: Performance hints for Unified Memory allow applications to provide CUDA with additional information to optimize decision-making, improving performance without impacting application semantics.

pages.title: Unified Memory Performance Tuning
pages.summary: General advice for achieving good performance with Unified Memory includes understanding paging, keeping data local, and tuning for memory transfer granularity, while noting that performance hints have associated costs.

pages.title: Unified Memory Programming Model
pages.summary: Unified Memory is a programming model that eliminates the need for separate host/device allocations and explicit memory transfers by allowing applications to allocate memory via system APIs, CUDA Managed Memory APIs, or managed variables.

pages.title: Runtime Detection of Unified Memory Support Level
pages.summary: This page explains how to detect Unified Memory support levels at runtime using the CUDA API attributes cudaDevAttrPageableMemoryAccess and cudaDevAttrConcurrentManagedAccess.

pages.title: Unified Memory Support Levels
pages.summary: CUDA Unified Memory support is categorized into four levels (Full, Managed Only, Managed Without Full Support, None) detectable via specific device properties such as pageableMemoryAccess, concurrentManagedAccess, and managedMemory.

pages.title: Difference between Unified Memory and Mapped Memory
pages.summary: CUDA Mapped Memory does not guarantee support for all memory operations like atomics on all systems, whereas Unified Memory does, though Mapped Memory is available on more systems.
aliases: CUDA Mapped Memory vs Unified Memory

pages.title: Unified Virtual Address Space (UVA)
pages.summary: Unified Virtual Address Space (UVA) allows 64-bit applications to use a single address space for host and device memory, enabling portable pointers and simplified memory management.

pages.title: Use Multicast Mappings
pages.summary: Multicast mappings enable collective memory operations in CUDA C++ using multimem PTX instructions, requiring compute capability 9.0 or higher.

pages.title: Variable Memory Space Specifiers
pages.summary: Overview of specifiers (__device__, __shared__, __constant__, etc.) that define the memory location of variables on the device.

pages.title: Virtual Aliasing Support
pages.summary: Virtual Memory Management APIs allow creating multiple virtual mappings (proxies) to the same allocation via cuMemMap, where consistency between proxies is enforced using fence.proxy.alias PTX instructions or stream/event synchronization.

pages.title: Volatile Qualifier
pages.summary: In CUDA C++, the volatile keyword is supported for ISO C++ compatibility but is unsuitable for inter-thread synchronization or MMIO because it does not guarantee atomicity, memory ordering, or deterministic instruction counts.

pages.title: wait and wait_prior
pages.summary: The `wait` and `wait_prior` collectives allow threads to wait for `memcpy_async` copies to complete. Both functions synchronize the named group.

pages.title: wait and wait_prior
pages.summary: Collectives that allow waiting for memcpy_async copies to complete, with wait blocking until all previous copies are done and wait_prior allowing the latest NumStages to still be in progress.

pages.title: Warp Matrix Functions (nvcuda::wmma)
pages.summary: C++ warp matrix operations leverage Tensor Cores to accelerate matrix problems of the form D=A*B+C, supported on mixed-precision floating point data for devices of compute capability 7.0 or higher.

pages.title: Warp Reduce Functions (__reduce_sync)
pages.summary: Intrinsics that perform reduction operations (add, min, max, and, or, xor) on data provided by threads in a mask after synchronization.

pages.title: Warp Shuffle Functions (__shfl_sync)
pages.summary: Intrinsics that exchange a variable between threads within a warp without use of shared memory, supported by devices of compute capability 5.0 or higher.

pages.title: __pipeline_arrive_on Primitive
pages.summary: The __pipeline_arrive_on primitive increments a shared memory barrier's arrival count, then decrements it once all preceding memcpy_async operations complete, resulting in a net zero change.

pages.title: __pipeline_commit Primitive
pages.summary: The __pipeline_commit function commits submitted memcpy_async operations to the pipeline as the current batch.

pages.title: __pipeline_memcpy_async Primitive
pages.summary: Documentation for the __pipeline_memcpy_async primitive, which requests an asynchronous copy operation. It details the parameters (dst_shared, src_global, size_and_align, zfill), the required alignment, and race condition warnings regarding source/destination memory access.

pages.title: __pipeline_wait_prior Primitive
pages.summary: The __pipeline_wait_prior primitive blocks execution until batches up to a specified index relative to the last committed batch have completed.

pages.title: cluster_group
pages.summary: The cluster_group class represents all threads in a single cluster (Compute Capability 9.0+) and provides synchronization, ranking, and shared memory mapping APIs.

pages.title: coalesced_group
pages.summary: Represents the set of active (coalesced) threads in a warp at a specific point in time, constructed via coalesced_threads() to enable cooperative operations on dynamically active thread subsets.

pages.title: Cooperative Groups API History
pages.summary: This page documents the history of changes to the Cooperative Groups API across CUDA versions, including the removal of multi_grid_group in CUDA 13.0, the addition of barrier functions in CUDA 12.2, and the promotion of experimental APIs in CUDA 12.0.

pages.title: CUDA TMA PTX Wrappers (cp.async.bulk.tensor)
pages.summary: C++ wrapper functions for the PTX `cp.async.bulk.tensor` instructions, providing interfaces for 1D through 5D bulk tensor asynchronous copies between global and shared memory.

pages.title: atomicAdd()
pages.summary: Performs an atomic addition on a value at a given memory address, returning the old value.

pages.title: atomicCAS()
pages.summary: Performs an atomic Compare And Swap operation on 16-bit, 32-bit, 64-bit, or 128-bit values in global or shared memory, returning the old value.

pages.title: atomicDec()
pages.summary: Performs an atomic decrement on a 32-bit unsigned integer in global or shared memory, returning the old value.

pages.title: atomicExch()
pages.summary: Performs an atomic exchange operation on global or shared memory, reading the old value, storing a new value, and returning the old value.

pages.title: atomicInc()
pages.summary: Performs an atomic increment on a 32-bit unsigned integer in global or shared memory, wrapping to zero if the value exceeds a limit.

pages.title: atomicMax()
pages.summary: Performs an atomic maximum operation on 32-bit or 64-bit integers in global or shared memory, returning the previous value.

pages.title: atomicMin()
pages.summary: Performs an atomic minimum operation on global or shared memory, returning the old value.

pages.title: atomicSub()
pages.summary: Performs an atomic subtraction on a 32-bit integer value in global or shared memory, returning the original value.

pages.title: CUDA C++11 Language Features
pages.summary: A comprehensive table of C++11 language features supported in nvcc device code, detailing specific proposals and their availability starting from CUDA 7.0.

pages.title: CUDA C++14 Language Features
pages.summary: This page details the C++14 language features supported in nvcc device code, including binary literals, generic lambdas, and variable templates, specifying the minimum nvcc version required for each.

pages.title: Compute Capability 10.0
pages.summary: Compute Capability 10.0 corresponds to the first-generation Blackwell architecture, featuring fifth-generation Tensor Cores, a unified 100 KB data cache/shared memory, and specialized matrix multiply-accumulate (MMA) features extending from Hopper.

pages.title: Compute Capability 12.0
pages.summary: Compute Capability 12.0 is associated with the Blackwell architecture (second gen) and B200 devices, though official documentation for its specific architectural features is currently superseded by legacy Driver API references.

pages.title: Compute Capability 5.x
pages.summary: Compute Capability 5.x devices, based on the Maxwell architecture, feature SMs with 128 CUDA cores, 32 SFUs, and 4 warp schedulers, along with a flexible memory hierarchy including configurable L1/texture cache and shared memory sizes.

pages.title: Compute Capability 6.x
pages.summary: Compute Capability 6.x devices, part of the Pascal architecture, feature SMs with varying core counts based on the specific sub-version (6.0, 6.1, 6.2), a unified L1/texture cache, and global/shared memory behaviors consistent with Compute Capability 5.x.

pages.title: Compute Capability 7.x
pages.summary: Compute Capability 7.x encompasses the Volta (7.0) and Turing (7.5) architectures, introducing Tensor Cores, Independent Thread Scheduling, and a unified data cache/shared memory partitioning system configurable via cudaFuncSetAttribute.

pages.title: Compute Capability 8.x
pages.summary: Compute Capability 8.x devices, part of the NVIDIA Ampere architecture, feature Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs) with varying core counts and unified data cache/shared memory configurations depending on the specific compute capability version.

pages.title: Compute Capability 9.0
pages.summary: Compute Capability 9.0 corresponds to the NVIDIA Hopper architecture, featuring advanced Streaming Multiprocessor (SM) designs with fifth-generation Tensor Cores supporting FP8 and other precision formats, a unified 256 KB data cache/shared memory hierarchy, and specialized matrix multiply-accumulate (MMA) acceleration features.

pages.title: CUDA Double-Precision Intrinsic Functions
pages.summary: A reference for CUDA double-precision floating-point intrinsic functions such as __dadd, __dsin, and __dmul, detailing their IEEE compliance, error bounds, and compute capability requirements.

pages.title: CUDA Double-Precision Mathematical Functions
pages.summary: A reference for CUDA double-precision floating-point mathematical functions, detailing their maximum Unit in the Last Place (ULP) error bounds and performance characteristics.

pages.title: cudaGraphAddMemAllocNode / cudaGraphAddMemFreeNode
pages.summary: Graph memory nodes allow explicit memory allocation and deallocation within a CUDA graph, requiring strict dependency ordering to ensure safe access to the allocated memory by kernel nodes.

pages.title: CUDA Graph Child Graph Ownership
pages.summary: CUDA 12.9 introduces the ability to move child graph ownership to a parent graph, allowing child graphs to contain memory allocation and free nodes while imposing strict restrictions on their subsequent usage.

pages.title: cudaGraphInstantiateFlagAutoFreeOnLaunch Example
pages.summary: Code example demonstrating the use of cudaGraphInstantiateFlagAutoFreeOnLaunch in a single-producer multiple-consumer algorithm to automatically manage memory lifetimes associated with captured graphs.

pages.title: cudaMemPoolExportPointer / cudaMemPoolImportPointer
pages.summary: These functions allow sharing memory pool allocations between processes via an opaque export data structure, requiring strict stream ordering and IPC event synchronization to ensure safe access and deallocation.

pages.title: cudaMemPoolExportToShareableHandle / cudaMemPoolImportFromShareableHandle
pages.summary: Sharing access to a memory pool involves retrieving an OS native handle via cudaMemPoolExportToShareableHandle(), transferring it using OS native IPC mechanisms, and creating an imported pool via cudaMemPoolImportFromShareableHandle().

pages.title: cudaMemPoolSetAccess
pages.summary: The cudaMemPoolSetAccess API enables access to imported memory pools from specific GPUs, as imported pools are initially only accessible from their resident device and do not inherit accessibility settings from the exporting process.

pages.title: cuda::ptx::tensormap_replace Functions
pages.summary: Functions wrapping the tensormap.replace PTX instruction to modify fields (address, rank, box dim, global dim, stride, element size, type, interleave, swizzle, fill mode) of a tiled-type tensor map on device.

pages.title: CUDA Quad-Precision Mathematical Functions
pages.summary: This page documents the __nv_fp128_* quad-precision mathematical functions available in CUDA, detailing their availability on compute capability 10.0+ devices and providing a table of functions with their maximum ULP error bounds.

pages.title: CUDA Single-Precision Intrinsic Functions
pages.summary: A reference for CUDA single-precision floating-point intrinsic functions, detailing their rounding modes, compiler behavior regarding FMAD fusion, and specific error bounds.

pages.title: CUDA Single-Precision Mathematical Functions
pages.summary: A reference for single-precision floating-point mathematical functions available in CUDA, detailing their usage in host and device code along with maximum Unit in Last Place (ULP) error bounds.

pages.title: CUDA Stream Capture Peer Access
pages.summary: This page details how CUDA stream capture records the peer accessibility state of memory pools at the moment of allocation, ensuring that subsequent changes to pool accessibility do not retroactively alter the graph's memory mappings.

pages.title: cudaLaunchKernelEx
pages.summary: An API for runtime kernel configuration that allows specifying thread block cluster dimensions via a cudaLaunchConfig_t structure.

pages.title: cudaDeviceGraphMemTrim
pages.summary: An API that explicitly releases physical memory reserved by inactive graph memory nodes back to the operating system.

pages.title: cudaGraphAddMemAllocNode
pages.summary: The cudaGraphAddMemAllocNode API adds a memory allocation node to a CUDA graph, allowing specification of the resident device and explicit access permissions for other devices via an access descriptor array.
aliases: Peer Access with Graph Node APIs

pages.title: cudaGraphUpload
pages.summary: A function that separates the cost of memory allocation and mapping from graph launch by performing mappings immediately and associating the graph with an upload stream, thereby optimizing first launch performance.

pages.title: cudaLaunchKernelEx
pages.summary: The `cudaLaunchKernelEx` API is used to launch a kernel with runtime configuration, specifically allowing for the specification of thread block cluster dimensions.

pages.title: cudaMallocManaged
pages.summary: An API for allocating CUDA Managed Memory that is syntactically identical to cudaMalloc.

pages.title: grid_group
pages.summary: The grid_group class represents all threads in a single grid, providing methods for querying grid topology and performing synchronization via the cooperative launch API.

pages.title: __ldg
pages.summary: The __ldg function reads data from global memory into the read-only data cache, supporting various scalar and vector types on devices with compute capability 5.0 or higher.

pages.title: Load Functions Using Cache Hints
pages.summary: Reference for load functions that use specific cache hints (__ldcg, __ldca, __ldcs, __ldlu, __ldcv). Supported on compute capability 5.0+.

pages.title: make_tensorsmap_template
pages.summary: An example function demonstrating how to create a minimal tiled-type tensor map template using the cuTensorMapEncodeTiled Driver API function, which can then be modified on device.

pages.title: __pipeline_arrive_on Primitive
pages.summary: The `__pipeline_arrive_on` function is a synchronization primitive used to manage barriers in shared memory for asynchronous memory copy operations.

pages.title: __pipeline_commit Primitive
pages.summary: The `__pipeline_commit` function is used to commit previously submitted `memcpy_async` operations to the pipeline, designating them as the current batch.

pages.title: __pipeline_memcpy_async Primitive
